 vote — did Prop 3 $104M tech pass?), verify Google Alert parser when ~April 9 digest arrives, then act on the 4 STRONG enriched signals (Tulsa PS, Richardson ISD, Acton-Boxborough, Norwalk PS) — research
 contacts and draft outreach

 ?

 ❯ I need a faster way to work thorugh and respond to my emails in my gmail inbox for steven@codecombat.com. As I scale up to send more emails I will need to build a system that works for me and my workflow to handle the increased incoming emails so I
 dont get backlogged and overwhelemed.

 I would love for there to be a way that everytime i opened up my inbox and clicked into an unread email, claude could draft up a response in my voice, that has no trace of AI, like em dashes or any AI tells. Id like the responses to be concise, human,
 empathetic, and direct. No cliche, coroporate, sales-y wording, or wide commonly used email phrasing. I'd like the drafts to match thte eneergy of the inbound - short reply to short email, longer to longer. And obviously, id like claude to be able
 to go through all my emails and search for commonly asked and aswered questions so that it has some konwledge of how i reply to those common requests and questions and can bring that knowledge to the drafts. As i tweak and send the drafts It would be
 great if claude could learn and get smarter overtime learning from my replies and those learnings coould compound overtime.

 Id assume a claude code + gmail mcp would be the way to go. i hear @gongrzhe/server-gmail-autoauth-mcp may be a good one to use.

 Thoughts?

 ⏺ Great idea — and you're closer to having this than you might think. Here are my thoughts:

 You already have Gmail MCP tools available in Claude Code — gmail_search_messages , gmail_read_message , gmail_read_thread , gmail_create_draft . That's the core read/write loop right there.

 ❯ Continue Session 48 for Scout. Read SCOUT_PLAN.md and CLAUDE.md for current state. Session 47 shipped 15 features (all live-tested): 6 new signal scanners, sequence re-engagement (report-then-act), lookalike

 prospecting, dormant detection, territory map heat overlay, and more. 22 of 24 strategies built — core infrastructure is COMPLETE. Shifting to operating mode. Immediate: check Tulsa PS bond results (April 7

 vote — did Prop 3 $104M tech pass?), verify Google Alert parser when ~April 9 digest arrives, then act on the 4 STRONG enriched signals (Tulsa PS, Richardson ISD, Acton-Boxborough, Norwalk PS) — research

 ⏺ Session 48 — current state loaded. 22 of 24 strategies built, signal system fully operational, shifting to operating mode.

 What do you want to do first?

 ❯ I need a faster way to work thorugh and respond to my emails in my gmail inbox for steven@codecombat.com. As I scale up to send more emails I will need to build a system that works for me and my workflow to handle the increased incoming emails so I

 dont get backlogged and overwhelemed.

 I would love for there to be a way that everytime i opened up my inbox and clicked into an unread email, claude could draft up a response in my voice, that has no trace of AI, like em dashes or any AI tells. Id like the responses to be concise, human,

 empathetic, and direct. No cliche, coroporate, sales-y wording, or wide commonly used email phrasing. I'd like the drafts to match thte eneergy of the inbound - short reply to short email, longer to longer. And obviously, id like claude to be able

 to go through all my emails and search for commonly asked and aswered questions so that it has some konwledge of how i reply to those common requests and questions and can bring that knowledge to the drafts. As i tweak and send the drafts It would be

 great if claude could learn and get smarter overtime learning from my replies and those learnings coould compound overtime.

 Id assume a claude code + gmail mcp would be the way to go. i hear @gongrzhe/server-gmail-autoauth-mcp may be a good one to use.

 Thoughts?

 ⏺ Great idea — and you're closer to having this than you might think. Here are my thoughts:

 You already have Gmail MCP tools available in Claude Code — gmail_search_messages , gmail_read_message , gmail_read_thread , gmail_create_draft . That's the core read/write loop right there.

 You also already have a voice profile from /train_voice (24 months of Gmail analysis) sitting in memory/voice_profile.md . That's a head start on the "sound like Steven" piece.

 The "auto-draft when I open an email" trigger — that's the hard part. True auto-trigger would need a Chrome extension or Gmail add-on, which is a whole separate project. But the practical version that actually works:

 - You open Claude Code, say something like "draft replies for my unread emails"

 - I pull unread messages, read the threads for context, draft replies in your voice

 - Drafts appear right in Gmail — you review, tweak, send

 - Batch 10-20 emails in minutes instead of one at a time

 For the learning loop:

 1. Phase 1 — Mine your history. Analyze your sent mail for common Q&A patterns (pricing questions, meeting requests, follow-ups, etc.) and build a response playbook

 2. Phase 2 — Draft with context. Use voice profile + playbook + thread context to generate drafts

 3. Phase 3 — Learn from edits. Compare what I drafted vs. what you actually sent — store the delta as feedback that compounds over time

 On the MCP choice: The @gongrzhe/server-gmail-autoauth-mcp may offer more granular control, but let's first check what the built-in Gmail MCP can do — it might be sufficient and it's already connected. If it's missing something (like batch operations

 or label management), we add the other one.

 My recommendation: Start right now by analyzing your sent emails to build the response playbook and voice rules. That's the foundation everything else builds on. Then we test-drive it on a batch of real unread emails today.

 Want to start with the sent email analysis, or do you want to talk through the workflow more first?

 The key unlock: gmail_create_draft supports threadId , so drafts appear as proper threaded replies in Gmail. You open your inbox, the drafts are already there waiting.

 Ready to code?

 Here is Claude's plan:
 ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌
 Plan: Email Reply Drafting Assistant

 Context

 zero AI tells. The system learns from his edits over time.

 Key discovery: The Gmail MCP already connected to Claude Code has everything we need — search, read threads, and create threaded drafts (drafts appear as proper replies in Gmail). No need for @gongrzhe MCP or any new MCP server.

 ---

 Phase 1: Build the Response Playbook (one-time, ~30 min)

 What: Analyze Steven's sent email history to extract categorized Q&A patterns.

 How: Use Gmail MCP right here in Claude Code to:

 1. Search in:sent emails (last 12 months, paginated)

 2. Read 150-200 reply threads to see incoming + Steven's response pairs

 3. Categorize into common patterns (pricing, scheduling, follow-up, technical, procurement, etc.)

 4. Extract Steven's typical response structure and real snippets for each category

 Output: memory/response_playbook.md — categorized response patterns with real examples

 Why not a Python module? This runs in Claude Code, not Scout. No need for agent/playbook_builder.py on Railway. Future rebuilds happen the same way — "rebuild my playbook" in Claude Code.

 ---

 Phase 2: Anti-AI-Tell Rules + Prompt Template (~15 min)

 2a. Update voice profile

 Add an "Anti-AI-Tell Checklist" section to memory/voice_profile.md :

 - Banned patterns: em dashes, semicolons, "I'd be happy to", "Absolutely!", "Great question!", "I hope this finds you well", "leverage/streamline/robust/comprehensive"

 - Banned structures: consecutive sentences starting with "I", overly parallel sentence structures, three-part lists with escalating adjectives

 - Required: match inbound energy/length, use Steven's actual phrases from the playbook

 2b. Create prompt template

 New file: prompts/reply_draft.md — the core template for drafting each reply:

 - Injects voice profile + matched playbook category + full thread context

 - Explicit anti-AI-tell rules in the prompt itself

 - Instructions to match inbound length/energy

 - Output: email body only (no subject, no signature — Gmail handles those)

 ---

 Phase 3: Document the Workflow in CLAUDE.md (~10 min)

 Add an "Email Reply Drafting Workflow" section so any Claude Code session knows how to execute:

 1. gmail_search_messages(q="is:unread in:inbox -category:promotions -category:social -from:noreply -from:no-reply -from:notifications")

 2. For each result: gmail_read_thread(threadId=...) to get full conversation context

 3. Skip: automated emails, newsletters, calendar invites, internal CC-only threads

 4. Load voice profile + response playbook

 5. Classify inbound against playbook categories

 6. Draft reply using prompts/reply_draft.md template

 7. Show Steven a summary of each draft (sender, subject, draft preview)

 8. On approval: gmail_create_draft(to=sender, body=draft, threadId=thread_id) — creates threaded draft in Gmail

 9. Log to memory/draft_log.md for the learning loop

 ---

 Phase 4: Test on Real Emails (~20 min)

 1. Run the workflow on 5 real unread emails

 2. Steven reviews each draft for voice accuracy and AI tells

 3. Iterate on the prompt template based on feedback

 4. Adjust playbook categories if gaps found

 ---

 Phase 5: Learning Loop (future sessions)

 5a. "Learn from my sends" workflow

 When Steven says "learn from my sends":

 1. Read memory/draft_log.md for recent drafts

 2. For each thread, check if Steven sent a reply via gmail_search_messages(q="in:sent")

 3. Compare draft vs what Steven actually sent

 4. Extract corrections (what he changed, why it matters)

 5. Append learnings to memory/voice_profile.md and memory/response_playbook.md

 5b. Explicit feedback

 Steven can say "that draft was too formal" or "grade that draft" — corrections go into voice profile (existing mechanism via VoiceTrainer.update_profile_from_feedback() ).

 5c. Periodic playbook rebuild

 Every 2-4 weeks: "rebuild my playbook" re-analyzes recent sent emails, refreshes categories and examples.

 ---

 Files

 ┌─────────────────────────────┬────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ File │ Action │ Purpose │

 ├─────────────────────────────┼────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ memory/response_playbook.md │ CREATE │ Categorized Q&A patterns from email history │

 ├─────────────────────────────┼────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ memory/draft_log.md │ CREATE │ Log of drafted replies (for learning loop) │

 ├─────────────────────────────┼────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ prompts/reply_draft.md │ CREATE │ Prompt template for drafting replies │

 ├─────────────────────────────┼────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ memory/voice_profile.md │ EDIT │ Add Anti-AI-Tell Checklist section │

 ├─────────────────────────────┼────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ CLAUDE.md │ EDIT │ Add Email Reply Drafting Workflow section │

 └─────────────────────────────┴────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 No Python modules needed. No Scout/Telegram changes. No Railway deploys. This is entirely a Claude Code workflow using existing Gmail MCP tools.

 ---

 Verification

 1. Run gmail_search_messages(q="is:unread in:inbox") — confirm MCP works

 2. Pick 5 real unread emails, draft replies, compare to how Steven would actually reply

 3. Create drafts in Gmail — confirm they appear threaded correctly

 4. Steven sends (or edits + sends) — run "learn from my sends" to verify the learning loop captures diffs

 ---

 - Playbook building: $0 (Gmail MCP is free, analysis happens in Claude Code conversation)

 - Daily drafting: $0 beyond normal Claude Code usage (drafting happens in conversation)

 - Learning loop: $0 beyond normal Claude Code usage

 - Total new API costs: $0

 ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌

 Claude has written up a plan and is ready to execute. Would you like to proceed?

 ❯ 1. Yes, auto-accept edits

 ❯ T ell Claude what to change

 l.abernathy@hcacrusaders.com

 T ell Claude what to change

 Refine this plan. Your job is to pressure-test it honestly — keep what's
 genuinely strong, rebuild what isn't, and hand back something I can execute
 with full confidence.
 Act as a ruthless, senior-level technical reviewer and architect. Be direct,

 specific, and opinionated. No hedging, no diplomacy, no soft language around

 real problems. If something is wrong, say it's wrong and say why. But the

 inverse also holds: if something is right, leave it alone. Do not rewrite solid

 work just to look thorough — churn for its own sake is its own kind of

 failure. The bar for changing something is that the new version is meaningfully

 better, not merely different.

 happen because the reviewer started reacting before they finished

 understanding.

 1. Challenge the foundation. Is the overall approach actually right, or is it

 just the first thing that came to mind? Interrogate the architecture, the

 framing, and the core assumptions before touching details. If there's a

 fundamentally better angle — different architecture, different sequencing,

 different tradeoffs, a smaller problem hiding inside the big one — say so and

 restructure around it. Do not anchor to your first answer because you already

 committed to it on the page.

 2. Stress-test every layer as if you're executing it tomorrow. Walk the plan

 end to end and ask: could I actually sit down and build this without hitting a

 wall? Find the hidden assumptions, the vague steps, the dependency tangles, the

 places where things will quietly break at the edges, the spots where "it'll

 just work" is doing real load-bearing work. For every problem: name it, explain

 why it happens, explain what it costs, and give the fix. Diagnosis without

 prescription is useless.

 3. Pressure-test every technical decision against the best available knowledge.

 Architecture, tooling, patterns, data modeling, sequencing — for each one, ask

 whether it's genuinely the strongest option or just the most familiar. Weigh

 it against what actually holds up in production, not what's popular in

 tutorials or trending this quarter. Where real tradeoffs exist between

 competing approaches, surface them honestly and take a clear position on which

 one I should pick and why. No "it depends" cop-outs.

 4. Optimize for the highest-quality result, reached as fast and efficiently as

 possible. Every step should be pulling its weight toward a correct, durable,

 high-quality outcome — not just toward motion. Ruthlessly cut anything that

 adds time without adding quality, and ruthlessly add anything that protects

 quality even if it costs a little time upfront. Sequence the work so that the

 decisions and pieces with the highest leverage on the final result come first,

 so that mistakes get caught as early and cheaply as possible, and so that

 nothing downstream has to be redone because something upstream was rushed.

 Where two paths exist, pick the one that gets to the correct answer with the

 least wasted motion — and say why. Speed comes from not doing the wrong work,

 not from skipping the right work.

 5. Cut the fat, keep the muscle. Depth is welcome. Redundancy, vagueness, and

 ceremony are not. Every element earns its place or it goes. If a step would

 make me stop and ask "what does this actually mean in practice," either make it

 concrete or delete it. If two steps do the same work, collapse them. If a

 section exists because it sounds thorough rather than because it changes the

 outcome, kill it.

 6. Fill the gaps a senior engineer would catch in a design review. What's

 missing that looks minor now and will hurt later? Edge cases, error handling,

 failure modes, prerequisites, migration paths, ordering constraints, and the

 easy-to-punt decisions that get expensive to reverse. Surface them now, while

 they're cheap.

 7. Rewrite the full plan from scratch. Do not patch the original — rebuild it

 with everything above baked in. Make it comprehensive, precise, and immediately

 actionable. Preserve the parts of the original that were already strong —

 rebuilding does not mean discarding what worked. The goal is the best possible

 plan, not the most rewritten one. Then, at the end, list the most important

 changes you made and explain the reasoning behind each one so I can judge

 whether your thinking holds up.

 Take your time. Think deeply. This is the version I'm going to execute from —

 make it the best one you're capable of producing.

 ❯ Continue Session 48 for Scout. Read SCOUT_PLAN.md and CLAUDE.md for current state. Session 47 shipped 15 features (all live-tested): 6 new signal scanners, sequence re-engagement (report-then-act), lookalike

 prospecting, dormant detection, territory map heat overlay, and more. 22 of 24 strategies built — core infrastructure is COMPLETE. Shifting to operating mode. Immediate: check Tulsa PS bond results (April 7

 vote — did Prop 3 $104M tech pass?), verify Google Alert parser when ~April 9 digest arrives, then act on the 4 STRONG enriched signals (Tulsa PS, Richardson ISD, Acton-Boxborough, Norwalk PS) — research

 contacts and draft outreach

 Before you begin making any changes or writing any code I have somethings i want to do.

 ⏺ Got it. Let me pull up the current state first, then I'm all ears.

 Read 1 file, recalled 1 memory (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Session 48 — current state loaded. 22 of 24 strategies built, signal system fully operational, shifting to operating mode.

 What do you want to do first?

 ❯ I need a faster way to work thorugh and respond to my emails in my gmail inbox for steven@codecombat.com. As I scale up to send more emails I will need to build a system that works for me and my workflow to handle the increased incoming emails so I

 dont get backlogged and overwhelemed.

 I would love for there to be a way that everytime i opened up my inbox and clicked into an unread email, claude could draft up a response in my voice, that has no trace of AI, like em dashes or any AI tells. Id like the responses to be concise, human,

 empathetic, and direct. No cliche, coroporate, sales-y wording, or wide commonly used email phrasing. I'd like the drafts to match thte eneergy of the inbound - short reply to short email, longer to longer. And obviously, id like claude to be able

 to go through all my emails and search for commonly asked and aswered questions so that it has some konwledge of how i reply to those common requests and questions and can bring that knowledge to the drafts. As i tweak and send the drafts It would be

 great if claude could learn and get smarter overtime learning from my replies and those learnings coould compound overtime.

 Id assume a claude code + gmail mcp would be the way to go. i hear @gongrzhe/server-gmail-autoauth-mcp may be a good one to use.

 Thoughts?

 ⏺ Great idea — and you're closer to having this than you might think. Here are my thoughts:

 You already have Gmail MCP tools available in Claude Code — gmail_search_messages , gmail_read_message , gmail_read_thread , gmail_create_draft . That's the core read/write loop right there.

 You also already have a voice profile from /train_voice (24 months of Gmail analysis) sitting in memory/voice_profile.md . That's a head start on the "sound like Steven" piece.

 The "auto-draft when I open an email" trigger — that's the hard part. True auto-trigger would need a Chrome extension or Gmail add-on, which is a whole separate project. But the practical version that actually works:

 - You open Claude Code, say something like "draft replies for my unread emails"

 - I pull unread messages, read the threads for context, draft replies in your voice

 - Drafts appear right in Gmail — you review, tweak, send

 - Batch 10-20 emails in minutes instead of one at a time

 For the learning loop:

 1. Phase 1 — Mine your history. Analyze your sent mail for common Q&A patterns (pricing questions, meeting requests, follow-ups, etc.) and build a response playbook

 2. Phase 2 — Draft with context. Use voice profile + playbook + thread context to generate drafts

 3. Phase 3 — Learn from edits. Compare what I drafted vs. what you actually sent — store the delta as feedback that compounds over time

 On the MCP choice: The @gongrzhe/server-gmail-autoauth-mcp may offer more granular control, but let's first check what the built-in Gmail MCP can do — it might be sufficient and it's already connected. If it's missing something (like batch operations

 or label management), we add the other one.

 My recommendation: Start right now by analyzing your sent emails to build the response playbook and voice rules. That's the foundation everything else builds on. Then we test-drive it on a batch of real unread emails today.

 Want to start with the sent email analysis, or do you want to talk through the workflow more first?

 ❯ This sounds good. I want you to enter plan mode and really think through this and create a plan

 ⎿  /plan to preview

 ⏺ Here's what I'm proposing:

 No new Python modules, no Scout changes, no Railway deploys. This is entirely a Claude Code workflow using the Gmail MCP tools you already have connected.

 5 phases:

 1. Build Response Playbook — Analyze your sent emails (last 12 months) to extract categorized Q&A patterns. Pricing questions, scheduling, follow-ups, technical, procurement, etc. with your real snippets and response structures. Saved to

 memory/response_playbook.md .

 2. Anti-AI-Tell Rules + Prompt Template — Add explicit banned patterns to your voice profile (em dashes, corporate phrases, consecutive "I" sentences). Create prompts/reply_draft.md as the core template.

 3. Document the Workflow — Add instructions to CLAUDE.md so any Claude Code session knows the flow: search unread → read threads → classify → draft → create threaded Gmail drafts.

 4. Test on Real Emails — Draft replies to 5 real unread emails, you review for voice accuracy and AI tells, we iterate.

 5. Learning Loop (future sessions) — "Learn from my sends" compares what I drafted vs what you actually sent. Corrections compound in the voice profile and playbook over time.

 Total new API costs: $0 — everything runs within Claude Code conversation + free Gmail MCP calls.

 The key unlock: gmail_create_draft supports threadId , so drafts appear as proper threaded replies in Gmail. You open your inbox, the drafts are already there waiting.

 ⏺ Good catch on something — the voice profile says Steven does use em dashes occasionally. The original plan banned them outright. That's wrong. Let me think through all the actual problems before rewriting.

 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── n______n

 Problems I found with the original plan:

 n______n
 ❯  ( ✦ ✦ )
 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ( oo )

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle) Knurlburst

 1. No verification first. The plan builds a playbook before even confirming the Gmail MCP works end-to-end. If thread reads fail or drafts don't thread correctly, everything downstream is wasted work.

 n______n
 ❯  ( ✦ ✦ )
 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ( oo )

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle) Knurlburst

 2. Playbook building is vague at the critical step. "Read 150-200 reply threads" in a single Claude Code session means potentially millions of tokens of thread context. No plan for how to handle that. Will hit context limits.

 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── n______n
 ❯  ✦ ✦
 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ( oo )

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle) Knurlburst

 - -

 ✦ ✦

 3. Missing email classification. Not every unread email needs a reply. Newsletters, CC'd threads, FYIs, notifications. And some emails NEED specific data (pricing, calendar, account info) that can't be faked. The plan treats all emails the same.

 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── n______n
 ❯  ✦ ✦
 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ( oo )

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle) Knurlburst

 ~ ~

 4. Em dash ban is factually wrong. Steven's own voice profile says he uses them "occasionally for asides." Banning them entirely would make drafts LESS like Steven, not more.
 ~ ~
 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── u______n
 ❯  ✦ ✦
 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ( oo )

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle) Knurlburst

 5. Draft quality degrades at scale. Processing 15-20 emails sequentially in one conversation fills context. By email #15, drafts get formulaic. No mitigation.

 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── n______n
 ❯  ✦ ✦
 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ( oo )

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle) Knurlburst

 6. Learning loop stores metadata but not draft text. Can't compare drafts to actual sends if you only logged thread_id and category.

 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── n______n

 7. CLAUDE.md is already 500+ lines. Adding a full workflow section bloats every session's context, even non-email ones.

 Here is Claude's plan:
 ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌
 Plan: Email Reply Drafting Assistant

 Context

 energy of each inbound. System learns from his edits over time.

 The Gmail MCP connected to Claude Code supports gmail_create_draft with threadId — drafts appear as proper threaded replies in Gmail. This is the core enabler. No new MCP servers needed.

 ---

 Step 1: Verify Gmail MCP End-to-End (~5 min)

 1. gmail_search_messages(q="is:unread in:inbox", maxResults=5) — confirm search returns message/thread IDs

 2. gmail_read_thread(threadId=<one from above>) — confirm full thread context comes back with sender, body, dates

 3. gmail_create_draft(to="test", body="test", threadId=<same>) — confirm draft appears threaded in Gmail

 4. Delete the test draft manually

 If any step fails, stop and debug before proceeding. The rest of the plan depends on this working.

 ---

 Step 2: Build Response Playbook (~30 min)

 What: Categorized Q&A patterns from Steven's real email history.

 Why the playbook matters: The voice profile captures HOW Steven writes (tone, structure, phrases). The playbook captures WHAT he writes for specific situations (pricing → include table + scheduling link, technical question → answer directly + offer

 call). Without it, drafts will sound like Steven but say the wrong things.

 How — two-pass approach to manage token limits:

 Pass 1: Survey (lightweight). Search sent emails to identify categories:

 gmail_search_messages(q="in:sent", maxResults=100)

 Scan subjects and snippets (NOT full bodies) to identify the major categories of emails Steven sends. Group into 8-12 categories. This is fast — just reading search result metadata.

 Pass 2: Deep dive (per category). For each category, read 5-8 representative full threads:

 gmail_search_messages(q="in:sent subject:<category keyword>", maxResults=8)

 gmail_read_thread(threadId=<each>)

 Extract: Steven's response structure, real snippets, typical CTA, length. This is ~60-80 thread reads total (8 categories x 8 threads), spread across focused searches. Each category is analyzed independently — no context accumulation.

 Output: memory/response_playbook.md with this structure:

 # Response Playbook

 ## Pricing / Licensing Questions

 **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, bulk, quote, PO

 **Steven's pattern:** Direct answer first line → pricing table → mention free content → soft CTA with scheduling link

 **Real snippets:**

 - "Our minimum purchase order is 10 annual licenses for $70 each ($700)."

 - [2-3 more]

 **Typical length:** Medium (8-15 lines with bullets/table)

 ## Scheduling / Meeting Requests

 ...

 ## [etc for each category]

 ## No Reply Needed (reference)

 **Types Steven doesn't reply to:** Newsletters, CC-only threads, automated notifications, FYI forwards

 The "No Reply Needed" section is critical — it teaches the system what to skip, not just what to draft.

 ---

 Step 3: Create Reply Draft Prompt + Update Voice Profile (~20 min)

 3a. prompts/reply_draft.md — the core drafting instructions

 This file contains everything Claude needs to draft a single reply. It gets loaded once per email-drafting session.

 # Email Reply Draft Instructions

 ## Voice Profile

 {voice _profile}

 ## Response Playbook Match

 {playbook_ category}

 ## Anti-AI-Tell Rules

 These patterns are the most common AI tells in email. Avoiding them is non-negotiable:

 - Do NOT use em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them rarely, only for brief asides.

 - Do NOT use "I'd be happy to", "I'd love to", "Absolutely!", "Great question!", "That's a great point"

 - Do NOT use "I hope this finds you well", "I wanted to reach out", "Just circling back"

 - Do NOT use "leverage", "streamline", "robust", "comprehensive", "cutting-edge", "foster", "delve"

 - Do NOT start 2+ consecutive sentences with "I"

 - Do NOT use semicolons (Steven never does)

 - Do NOT use three-part lists with escalating adjectives ("fast, efficient, and powerful")

 - Do NOT be more enthusiastic than the sender. Match their energy, don't amplify it.

 - DO use Steven's actual phrases: "Let me know if you need anything else", "Happy to help", "No worries", "Sounds good", "Want to [action]?"

 - DO vary sentence length — mix short punchy with medium explanatory, like conversation

 - DO end emails naturally — Steven often just stops after the last point, no formal sign-off

 ## Energy Matching

 - 1-2 sentence inbound → 1-3 sentence reply

 - Casual tone → casual reply ("Hey", tilde, emoji if appropriate)

 - Formal/procurement → structured but still warm

 - Frustrated → empathetic first, then solution

 ## Email Classification (decide BEFORE drafting)

 - **DRAFT** : Standard Q&A, follow-up, scheduling, acknowledgment → draft a reply

 - **FLAG** : Needs specific data Steven must provide (custom quote, calendar availability, account-specific info) → draft what you can, mark [STEVEN: need X] inline

 - **SKIP** : Newsletter, notification, CC-only, FYI, automated → don't draft, log as skipped

 ## Output Format

 Email body only. No subject line. No signature. No explanation.

 3b. Update memory/voice_profile.md

 Add one section at the bottom (before the Summary):

 ## Anti-AI-Tell Checklist (added 2026-04-07)

 When drafting AS Steven, avoid these common AI patterns that break the illusion:

 - Em dashes as clause connectors (Steven uses them rarely, only for brief asides — not as default punctuation)

 - Semicolons (never)

 - "I'd be happy to" / "I'd love to" / "Absolutely!" / "Great question!"

 - "I hope this finds you well" / "Just wanted to reach out"

 - Corporate vocabulary: leverage, streamline, robust, comprehensive, foster, delve

 - Starting consecutive sentences with "I"

 - Being more enthusiastic than the sender

 - Three-part escalating lists

 - Overly parallel sentence structures

 ---

 Step 4: Document Workflow in CLAUDE.md (~5 min)

 Keep it brief — CLAUDE.md is already 500+ lines and loaded every session. Add a short section that points to the prompt file:

 ### Email Reply Drafting (Claude Code workflow)

 When Steven says "draft my emails", "check my inbox", or "draft replies":

 1. Load `prompts/reply_draft.md` and `memory/response_playbook.md`

 2. Search: `gmail_search_messages(q="is:unread in:inbox -category:promotions -category:social -from:noreply -from:no-reply")`

 3. For each email: `gmail_read_thread(threadId=...)` → classify (DRAFT/FLAG/SKIP per reply _draft.md) → draft if applicable

 4. Show Steven: sender, subject, draft preview, and classification for each email

 5. Process in batches of 5-7 to prevent quality degradation from context accumulation

 6. On approval: `gmail_ create _draft(to=sender, body=draft, threadId=thread_ id)`

 7. Log to `memory/draft_log.md`

 Detailed rules in `prompts/reply_draft.md`. Response patterns in `memory/response_playbook.md`.

 ---

 Step 5: Test on Real Emails (~20 min)

 Test on 5 real unread emails, deliberately picking a mix:

 - One short/casual inbound (should get a short reply)

 - One pricing/product question (should match playbook pattern)

 - One that doesn't need a reply (should be classified SKIP)

 - One that needs Steven's specific input (should be classified FLAG)

 - One mid-thread reply (should account for full thread context)

 For each draft, Steven evaluates:

 1. Does this sound like me? (voice accuracy)

 2. Any AI tells? (the smell test)

 3. Is the content right? (correct category, right info)

 4. Right length? (energy matching)

 Iterate on prompts/reply_draft.md based on feedback. This is where the prompt gets battle-tested.

 ---

 Step 6: Learning Loop Setup (~10 min)

 Draft log format ( memory/draft_log.md )

 Must store actual draft text — not just metadata — so we can compare later:

 ## 2026-04-07

 ### thread:18f2a3b4c5d

 - from: kent@austinisd.org

 - subject: Re: CodeCombat Pricing

 - category: pricing _question

 - status: drafted

 - draft: |

 Hey Kent,

 Our minimum is 10 annual licenses at $70 each ($700). Here's the full breakdown:

 [...]

 ### thread:18f2a3b4c5e

 - from: notifications@outreach.io

 - status: skipped (automated)

 "Learn from my sends" workflow

 When Steven says "learn from my sends" in a future session:

 1. Read memory/draft_log.md for entries from last 7 days with status: drafted

 2. For each thread_id: gmail_search_messages(q="in:sent") filtered to that thread

 3. If Steven sent a reply in that thread:

 - Compare draft text (from log) to sent text (from Gmail)

 - If substantially the same → positive signal, no action needed

 - If different → extract what changed (tone shift? content added? shortened? different CTA?)

 - Append correction to memory/voice_profile.md under a "Learned Corrections" section

 - If category-specific, also update memory/response_playbook.md

 4. Report: "You sent 12 of 15 drafts. 8 unchanged, 4 edited. Learnings: [summary]"

 This compounds. After a month of daily use, the voice profile and playbook have dozens of real corrections baked in.

 ---

 Files

 │ File │ Action │ Purpose │

 ├─────────────────────────────┼────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 No Python modules. No Scout/Telegram changes. No Railway deploys.

 ---

 Verification

 1. Gmail MCP works: Search, read thread, create threaded draft — all succeed

 2. Playbook quality: Categories cover 80%+ of Steven's typical inbound

 3. Draft quality: 5 test emails pass Steven's voice/AI-tell/content review

 4. Threading works: Drafts appear as proper replies in Gmail (not new threads)

 5. Learning loop: After Steven sends edited drafts, "learn from my sends" correctly identifies changes

 ---

 Incremental Cost: $0

 All processing happens within Claude Code conversation. Gmail MCP calls are free. No external API calls (Serper, Claude API, etc.) needed — Claude Code IS the LLM.

 [<u[>1u[>4;2m

 Refine this plan. Your job is to pressure-test it
 honestly — keep what's genuinely strong, rebuild what isn't, and hand back
 something I can execute with full confidence.
 Act as a ruthless, senior-level technical reviewer and architect. Be direct,

 specific, and opinionated. No hedging, no diplomacy, no soft language around

 real problems. If something is wrong, say it's wrong and say why. But the

 inverse also holds: if something is right, leave it alone. Do not rewrite solid

 work just to look thorough — churn for its own sake is its own kind of

 failure. The bar for changing something is that the new version is meaningfully

 better, not merely different.

 happen because the reviewer started reacting before they finished

 understanding.

 1. Challenge the foundation. Is the overall approach actually right, or is it

 just the first thing that came to mind? Interrogate the architecture, the

 framing, and the core assumptions before touching details. If there's a

 fundamentally better angle — different architecture, different sequencing,

 different tradeoffs, a smaller problem hiding inside the big one — say so and

 restructure around it. Do not anchor to your first answer because you already

 committed to it on the page.

 2. Stress-test every layer as if you're executing it tomorrow. Walk the plan

 end to end and ask: could I actually sit down and build this without hitting a

 wall? Find the hidden assumptions, the vague steps, the dependency tangles, the

 places where things will quietly break at the edges, the spots where "it'll

 just work" is doing real load-bearing work. For every problem: name it, explain

 why it happens, explain what it costs, and give the fix. Diagnosis without

 prescription is useless.

 3. Pressure-test every technical decision against the best available knowledge.

 Architecture, tooling, patterns, data modeling, sequencing — for each one, ask

 whether it's genuinely the strongest option or just the most familiar. Weigh

 it against what actually holds up in production, not what's popular in

 tutorials or trending this quarter. Where real tradeoffs exist between

 competing approaches, surface them honestly and take a clear position on which

 one I should pick and why. No "it depends" cop-outs.

 4. Optimize for the highest-quality result, reached as fast and efficiently as

 possible. Every step should be pulling its weight toward a correct, durable,

 high-quality outcome — not just toward motion. Ruthlessly cut anything that

 adds time without adding quality, and ruthlessly add anything that protects

 quality even if it costs a little time upfront. Sequence the work so that the

 decisions and pieces with the highest leverage on the final result come first,

 so that mistakes get caught as early and cheaply as possible, and so that

 nothing downstream has to be redone because something upstream was rushed.

 Where two paths exist, pick the one that gets to the correct answer with the

 least wasted motion — and say why. Speed comes from not doing the wrong work,

 not from skipping the right work.

 5. Cut the fat, keep the muscle. Depth is welcome. Redundancy, vagueness, and

 ceremony are not. Every element earns its place or it goes. If a step would

 make me stop and ask "what does this actually mean in practice," either make it

 concrete or delete it. If two steps do the same work, collapse them. If a

 section exists because it sounds thorough rather than because it changes the

 outcome, kill it.

 6. Fill the gaps a senior engineer would catch in a design review. What's

 missing that looks minor now and will hurt later? Edge cases, error handling,

 failure modes, prerequisites, migration paths, ordering constraints, and the

 easy-to-punt decisions that get expensive to reverse. Surface them now, while

 they're cheap.

 7. Rewrite the full plan from scratch. Do not patch the original — rebuild it

 with everything above baked in. Make it comprehensive, precise, and immediately

 actionable. Preserve the parts of the original that were already strong —

 rebuilding does not mean discarding what worked. The goal is the best possible

 plan, not the most rewritten one. Then, at the end, list the most important

 changes you made and explain the reasoning behind each one so I can judge

 whether your thinking holds up.

 Take your time. Think deeply. This is the version I'm going to execute from —

 make it the best one you're capable of producing.

 What do you want to do first?

 ❯ I need a faster way to work thorugh and respond to my emails in my gmail inbox for steven@codecombat.com. As I scale up to send more emails I will need to build a system that works for me and my workflow to handle the increased incoming emails so I

 dont get backlogged and overwhelemed.

 I would love for there to be a way that everytime i opened up my inbox and clicked into an unread email, claude could draft up a response in my voice, that has no trace of AI, like em dashes or any AI tells. Id like the responses to be concise, human,

 empathetic, and direct. No cliche, coroporate, sales-y wording, or wide commonly used email phrasing. I'd like the drafts to match thte eneergy of the inbound - short reply to short email, longer to longer. And obviously, id like claude to be able

 to go through all my emails and search for commonly asked and aswered questions so that it has some konwledge of how i reply to those common requests and questions and can bring that knowledge to the drafts. As i tweak and send the drafts It would be

 great if claude could learn and get smarter overtime learning from my replies and those learnings coould compound overtime.

 Id assume a claude code + gmail mcp would be the way to go. i hear @gongrzhe/server-gmail-autoauth-mcp may be a good one to use.

 Thoughts?

 ⏺ Great idea — and you're closer to having this than you might think. Here are my thoughts:

 You already have Gmail MCP tools available in Claude Code — gmail_search_messages , gmail_read_message , gmail_read_thread , gmail_create_draft . That's the core read/write loop right there.

 You also already have a voice profile from /train_voice (24 months of Gmail analysis) sitting in memory/voice_profile.md . That's a head start on the "sound like Steven" piece.

 The "auto-draft when I open an email" trigger — that's the hard part. True auto-trigger would need a Chrome extension or Gmail add-on, which is a whole separate project. But the practical version that actually works:

 - You open Claude Code, say something like "draft replies for my unread emails"

 - I pull unread messages, read the threads for context, draft replies in your voice

 - Drafts appear right in Gmail — you review, tweak, send

 - Batch 10-20 emails in minutes instead of one at a time

 For the learning loop:

 1. Phase 1 — Mine your history. Analyze your sent mail for common Q&A patterns (pricing questions, meeting requests, follow-ups, etc.) and build a response playbook

 2. Phase 2 — Draft with context. Use voice profile + playbook + thread context to generate drafts

 3. Phase 3 — Learn from edits. Compare what I drafted vs. what you actually sent — store the delta as feedback that compounds over time

 On the MCP choice: The @gongrzhe/server-gmail-autoauth-mcp may offer more granular control, but let's first check what the built-in Gmail MCP can do — it might be sufficient and it's already connected. If it's missing something (like batch operations

 or label management), we add the other one.

 My recommendation: Start right now by analyzing your sent emails to build the response playbook and voice rules. That's the foundation everything else builds on. Then we test-drive it on a batch of real unread emails today.

 Want to start with the sent email analysis, or do you want to talk through the workflow more first?

 ⎿   Done (27 tool uses · 55.0k tokens · 2m 27s)

 ⏺ Good design work. Let me read the key files to validate a few things before finalizing the plan.

 Read 2 files (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⎿  Loaded agent/CLAUDE.md

 ⏺ Let me check what the Gmail MCP tools can actually do.

 ⏺ Now I have the full picture. The Gmail MCP is powerful — notably gmail_create_draft supports threadId so drafts appear properly threaded in Gmail. Let me write the plan.

 ⏺ Updated plan

 ⎿  /plan to preview

 ⏺ Here's what I'm proposing:

 No new Python modules, no Scout changes, no Railway deploys. This is entirely a Claude Code workflow using the Gmail MCP tools you already have connected.

 5 phases:

 1. Build Response Playbook — Analyze your sent emails (last 12 months) to extract categorized Q&A patterns. Pricing questions, scheduling, follow-ups, technical, procurement, etc. with your real snippets and response structures. Saved to

 memory/response_playbook.md .

 2. Anti-AI-Tell Rules + Prompt Template — Add explicit banned patterns to your voice profile (em dashes, corporate phrases, consecutive "I" sentences). Create prompts/reply_draft.md as the core template.

 3. Document the Workflow — Add instructions to CLAUDE.md so any Claude Code session knows the flow: search unread → read threads → classify → draft → create threaded Gmail drafts.

 4. Test on Real Emails — Draft replies to 5 real unread emails, you review for voice accuracy and AI tells, we iterate.

 5. Learning Loop (future sessions) — "Learn from my sends" compares what I drafted vs what you actually sent. Corrections compound in the voice profile and playbook over time.

 Total new API costs: $0 — everything runs within Claude Code conversation + free Gmail MCP calls.

 The key unlock: gmail_create_draft supports threadId , so drafts appear as proper threaded replies in Gmail. You open your inbox, the drafts are already there waiting.

 ⎿  Tool use rejected

 ⏺ Let me actually think about this before rewriting.

 Read 2 files (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Good catch on something — the voice profile says Steven does use em dashes occasionally. The original plan banned them outright. That's wrong. Let me think through all the actual problems before rewriting.

 Problems I found with the original plan:

 1. No verification first. The plan builds a playbook before even confirming the Gmail MCP works end-to-end. If thread reads fail or drafts don't thread correctly, everything downstream is wasted work.

 2. Playbook building is vague at the critical step. "Read 150-200 reply threads" in a single Claude Code session means potentially millions of tokens of thread context. No plan for how to handle that. Will hit context limits.

 3. Missing email classification. Not every unread email needs a reply. Newsletters, CC'd threads, FYIs, notifications. And some emails NEED specific data (pricing, calendar, account info) that can't be faked. The plan treats all emails the same.

 4. Em dash ban is factually wrong. Steven's own voice profile says he uses them "occasionally for asides." Banning them entirely would make drafts LESS like Steven, not more.

 5. Draft quality degrades at scale. Processing 15-20 emails sequentially in one conversation fills context. By email #15, drafts get formulaic. No mitigation.

 6. Learning loop stores metadata but not draft text. Can't compare drafts to actual sends if you only logged thread_id and category.

 7. CLAUDE.md is already 500+ lines. Adding a full workflow section bloats every session's context, even non-email ones.

 ⏺ Updated plan

 ⎿  /plan to preview

 ⏺ Here's the rebuilt plan. Key changes from the original and why:

 1. Verification first (was buried in Phase 4). The entire plan depends on Gmail MCP's threadId drafting working correctly. If it doesn't, everything is wasted. Now it's Step 1.

 2. Two-pass playbook building (was vague "read 150-200 threads"). Reading 200 full threads in one session would blow context limits. Now it's: Pass 1 surveys subjects/snippets (lightweight), Pass 2 deep-dives 5-8 threads per category (focused,

 manageable). ~60-80 total thread reads, each analyzed independently.

 3. Email classification system (was missing entirely). Added DRAFT/FLAG/SKIP classification. "Send me a quote" can't be replied to with just voice matching — it gets flagged with [STEVEN: need X] inline. Newsletters get skipped. This prevents the

 system from generating wrong or empty replies.

 4. Em dash rule corrected (was factually wrong). Steven's own voice profile says he uses them "occasionally for asides." Changed from "ban completely" to "don't use as clause connectors like AI does."

 5. Batch size limits (was missing). Added "process in batches of 5-7" to prevent quality degradation from context accumulation. By email #15 in a single conversation, drafts get formulaic.

 6. Draft log stores actual text (was metadata-only). The learning loop can't compare drafts to sends if it only stored thread_id and category.

 7. CLAUDE.md section is 10 lines, not a full workflow (was going to bloat an already 500+ line file). Detailed rules live in prompts/reply_draft.md where they belong.

 ⎿  Tool use rejected

 Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ I've been sitting with the full picture. Here's what I'm actually changing and why, then the rebuilt plan.

 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── n______n
 ❯  ( ✦ ✦ )
 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── oo
 ⏸ plan mode on (shif +tab to cycle) Knurlburst

 Foundational challenge I considered and rejected: Replacing the static playbook with dynamic few-shot retrieval (search Gmail for similar past replies at draft time). It's architecturally elegant but wrong for this problem. Steven's inbound
 categories are predictable — pricing, scheduling, follow-ups, technical, procurement. A static playbook covering 8-10 categories handles 90%+ of his inbox. Dynamic retrieval adds latency per email, matches might be poor, and it's more complex to
 execute. The playbook is correct.

 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── n______n

 ❯  ( ✦ ✦ )

 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ( oo )

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle) Knurlburst

 Structural issue I'm fixing: V2 puts voice rules in three places — voice_profile.md , the anti-AI-tell section added to voice_profile.md , AND prompts/reply_draft.md . That's fragmented. Voice rules belong in the voice profile. The prompt file should
 contain WORKFLOW instructions only (how to classify, draft, and output). Clean separation: voice profile = how Steven sounds. Playbook = what Steven says. Prompt file = how the drafting process works.

 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── n______n
 ❯  ( ✦ ✦ )

 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ( oo )

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle) Knurlburst

 Missing quality gate: V2's testing step has no pass/fail criteria. If the first 5 drafts are mostly bad, continuing to build the learning loop is wasted work. Need an explicit gate: if <3 of 5 are usable without significant rewriting, stop and
 iterate before proceeding.

 Here is Claude's plan:
 ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌
 Plan: Email Reply Drafting Assistant

 Context

 learns from his edits over time.

 The Gmail MCP already connected to Claude Code has gmail_create_draft with threadId — drafts appear as proper threaded replies. No new MCP servers or Python modules needed.

 ---

 Step 1: Verify Gmail MCP (~5 min)

 1. gmail_search_messages(q="is:unread in:inbox", maxResults=5) — confirm search returns message IDs and thread IDs

 2. gmail_read_thread(threadId=<one from above>) — confirm it returns all messages with sender, body, dates

 3. gmail_create_draft(to="steven@codecombat.com", body="test draft - delete me", threadId=<same>) — confirm draft appears threaded in Gmail

 4. Verify the draft shows the correct "from" address (steven@codecombat.com)

 5. Steven deletes the test draft

 Failure mode: If threadId drafting doesn't thread correctly, fall back to creating non-threaded drafts with the subject line pre-filled as "Re: [original subject]". Less elegant but functional. If the MCP can't create drafts at all, fall back to the

 existing GAS bridge create_draft() method — but we lose threading.

 ---

 Step 2: Build Response Playbook (~20 min)

 The voice profile captures HOW Steven writes. The playbook captures WHAT he writes for specific situations — the content patterns that vary by email type.

 Two-pass approach:

 Pass 1 — Category discovery (lightweight).

 gmail_search_messages(q="in:sent -subject:Re:", maxResults=50) # cold emails

 gmail_search_messages(q="in:sent subject:Re:", maxResults=100) # replies

 Scan subjects and snippets (not full bodies) to identify Steven's 8-12 most common email categories. Expected categories: pricing/licensing, scheduling/demos, follow-up after demo, technical/how-to, procurement/PO, renewals, pilot offers, free

 content/resources, brief acknowledgments, referral/introduction.

 Pass 2 — Pattern extraction (per category).

 For each category, search for 5-8 representative threads using topic keywords:

 gmail_search_messages(q="in:sent pricing OR license OR quote", maxResults=8)

 gmail_read_thread(threadId=<each>)

 From each thread, extract: Steven's response structure, real snippets, typical CTA, and length. ~50-70 total thread reads across all categories.

 Output: memory/response_playbook.md

 # Response Playbook

 ## Pricing / Licensing

 **Trigger:** Cost, per-seat, bulk, quote, PO

 **Steven's pattern:** Direct price answer first → pricing table/breakdown → mention free content included → soft CTA with scheduling link

 **Real snippets:**

 - "Our minimum purchase order is 10 annual licenses for $70 each ($700)."

 - [2-3 more actual quotes from his emails]

 **Typical length:** Medium (8-15 lines, uses bullet/table for prices)

 ## Scheduling / Demo Requests

 [same structure]

 ## [6-10 more categories]

 ## No Reply Needed (reference)

 These types should be SKIPPED — Steven does not reply to:

 - Automated notifications (Outreach, Salesforce, PandaDoc, Dialpad, calendar)

 - Newsletters and marketing emails

 - CC-only threads where Steven isn't addressed directly

 - Sales/recruiting outreach TO Steven

 - Internal company-wide announcements

 - Support ticket auto-responses

 ---

 Step 3: Update Voice Profile + Create Workflow File (~15 min)

 3a. Add Anti-AI-Tell section to memory/voice_profile.md

 Append before the Summary section:

 ## Anti-AI-Tell Checklist

 These are the most common patterns that make AI-drafted emails detectable. Violating any of these breaks the illusion:

 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 - Semicolons. Steven never uses them.

 - "I'd be happy to" / "I'd love to" / "Absolutely!" / "Great question!" / "That's a great point"

 - "I hope this finds you well" / "I wanted to reach out" / "Just circling back" / "per our conversation"

 - Corporate vocabulary: leverage, streamline, robust, comprehensive, cutting-edge, foster, delve, facilitate, utilize

 - Starting 2+ consecutive sentences with "I"

 - Three-part escalating lists ("fast, efficient, and powerful")

 - Being more enthusiastic than the sender

 - Overly parallel sentence structures

 - Filler transitions: "Additionally," "Furthermore," "Moreover," "In terms of"

 This lives in the voice profile because it's about Steven's voice — not about workflow.

 3b. Create prompts/reply_draft.md — workflow instructions

 This file tells Claude Code HOW to execute the drafting process. It does NOT contain voice rules (those are in voice_profile.md).

 # Email Reply Drafting — Workflow Instructions

 ## When to run

 Steven says "draft my emails", "check my inbox", "draft replies", or similar.

 ## Before starting

 Load these files into context:

 - memory/voice _profile.md (voice rules + anti-AI-tell checklist)

 - memory/response_ playbook.md (category patterns + real snippets)

 ## Step 1: Find emails needing replies

 gmail _search_ messages(q="is:unread in:inbox -category:promotions -category:social -from:noreply -from:no-reply -from:notifications -from:mailer-daemon")

 ## Step 2: Read and classify each email

 For each result, read the full thread: gmail _read_ thread(threadId=...)

 Classify into one of three buckets:

 **DRAFT** — Standard email Steven can reply to with voice + playbook knowledge.

 Examples: pricing questions, scheduling, follow-ups, acknowledgments, product questions.

 **FLAG** — Needs specific info only Steven has, or requires a judgment call.

 Draft what you can and mark gaps inline: [STEVEN: insert availability / custom quote / specific decision].

 Examples: "What's your availability next week?", custom enterprise pricing, contract negotiations, complaints.

 **SKIP** — No reply needed or out of scope.

 - Automated notifications (Outreach, Salesforce, PandaDoc, Dialpad, calendar)

 - Newsletters, marketing, promotions

 - CC-only threads where Steven isn't directly addressed

 - Sales outreach or recruiting emails TO Steven

 - Internal company-wide emails

 - Support ticket auto-responses

 - Emails already replied to (check if Steven's address appears in later thread messages)

 ## Step 3: Draft replies

 For each DRAFT or FLAG email:

 1. Match to the closest playbook category

 2. Write the reply body following voice _profile.md rules (especially Anti-AI-Tell)

 3. Match inbound energy: short email → short reply, casual → casual, formal → warm-but-structured

 4. Body only — no subject line (Gmail handles Re:), no signature (auto-appended by Gmail)

 5. Start with greeting, end naturally (Steven often just stops, no sign-off needed)

 ## Step 4: Present for review

 Show Steven each email in this format:

 ---

 **From:** sender name < email >

 **Subject:** Re: subject line

 **Classification:** DRAFT | FLAG

 **Draft:**

 [the draft reply text]

 ---

 Steven approves, edits, or skips each one.

 ## Step 5: Create Gmail drafts

 For approved emails:

 gmail_ create _draft(to=sender_ email, body=approved _text, threadId=thread_ id, cc=original _cc_ recipients)

 CC handling: Include all original CC recipients from the inbound email, minus any noreply/automated addresses. If the inbound was sent to multiple people including Steven, reply-all.

 ## Step 6: Log for learning

 Append to memory/draft _log.md (see format below).

 ---

 Step 4: Add Workflow Reference to CLAUDE.md (~2 min)

 Brief section — CLAUDE.md is already 500+ lines. Just a pointer:

 ### Email Reply Drafting (Claude Code workflow)

 When Steven says "draft my emails" or "check my inbox": load and follow `prompts/reply_draft.md`.

 Voice rules in `memory/voice_profile.md`. Response patterns in `memory/response_playbook.md`.

 Draft log in `memory/draft_log.md`. See prompts/reply _draft.md for the full workflow.

 ---

 Step 5: Test on 5 Real Emails (~15 min)

 Pick a deliberate mix:

 - 1 short/casual inbound (should produce a short reply)

 - 1 pricing or product question (should match playbook, include real data)

 - 1 that doesn't need a reply (should be classified SKIP)

 - 1 that needs Steven-specific input (should be classified FLAG with inline markers)

 - 1 mid-thread with multiple prior messages (should account for thread context)

 Steven evaluates each draft on 4 criteria:

 1. Voice — Does this sound like me?

 2. AI tells — Any phrases or patterns that smell like AI?

 3. Content — Is the information right? Right category match?

 4. Energy — Right length and tone for this specific email?

 Quality Gate

 If 3+ of 5 drafts are usable (Steven can send with minor tweaks): proceed to Step 6.

 If <3 of 5 are usable: Stop. Identify what's failing (voice? content? classification?) and iterate on the prompt/playbook/voice profile before doing more. Don't build the learning loop on a broken drafting foundation.

 ---

 Step 6: Learning Loop (~10 min to set up)

 Draft log format ( memory/draft_log.md )

 Stores draft text so future sessions can compare against what Steven actually sent:

 # Draft Log

 ## 2026-04-07 09:15

 ### thread:abc123

 from: kent@austinisd.org | subject: Re: CodeCombat Pricing | category: pricing

 DRAFT:

 Hey Kent,

 Our minimum is 10 annual licenses at $70 each ($700). Here's the full breakdown:

 [rest of draft]

 ### thread:def456

 from: daphne@hisd.org | subject: Re: Demo follow-up | category: follow _up

 DRAFT:

 Hey Daphne,

 Sounds good! Here's my scheduling link: [link]

 Cleanup: At the start of each drafting session, delete entries older than 14 days. The log should never exceed ~50 entries.

 "Learn from my sends" workflow

 When Steven says "learn from my sends" (or at the start of a drafting session as a quick pre-check):

 1. Read memory/draft_log.md for recent entries

 2. For each thread_id: gmail_read_thread(threadId=...) to check if Steven sent a reply

 3. Three outcomes per thread:

 - Sent unchanged (or near-identical): Positive signal. No action.

 - Sent with edits : Extract what changed and why. Append correction to voice_profile.md under a "Learned Corrections" section. If category-specific, also update the playbook.

 - Never sent (Steven wrote his own reply or skipped): Flag as a miss. Analyze what was wrong.

 4. Report summary: "12 of 15 drafts sent. 8 unchanged, 3 edited, 1 skipped. Learned: [specific corrections]."

 Over time, the voice profile and playbook accumulate real corrections from Steven's behavior. After a month of daily use, the system has 20-30 corrections that compound into substantially better drafts.

 ---

 ┌─────────────────────────────┬────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ File │ Action │ Purpose │

 ├─────────────────────────────┼────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 ├─────────────────────────────┼────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 └─────────────────────────────┴────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 No Python modules. No Scout/Telegram changes. No Railway deploys. No new MCP servers.

 ---

 Verification Checklist

 1. Gmail MCP search/read/draft all work end-to-end

 2. Threaded drafts appear correctly in Gmail (not as new threads)

 3. CC recipients are preserved on reply-all

 4. Playbook covers Steven's 8-10 most common email categories

 5. Quality gate passed: 3+ of 5 test drafts usable with minor tweaks

 6. Draft log captures text correctly for learning loop

 7. "Learn from my sends" correctly identifies edits vs unchanged sends

 Cost: $0

 All processing is within the Claude Code conversation. Gmail MCP calls are free. No external API costs.

 What do you want to do first?

 ❯ I need a faster way to work thorugh and respond to my emails in my gmail inbox for steven@codecombat.com. As I scale up to send more emails I will need to build a system that works for me and my workflow to handle the increased incoming emails so I

 dont get backlogged and overwhelemed.

 I would love for there to be a way that everytime i opened up my inbox and clicked into an unread email, claude could draft up a response in my voice, that has no trace of AI, like em dashes or any AI tells. Id like the responses to be concise, human,

 empathetic, and direct. No cliche, coroporate, sales-y wording, or wide commonly used email phrasing. I'd like the drafts to match thte eneergy of the inbound - short reply to short email, longer to longer. And obviously, id like claude to be able

 to go through all my emails and search for commonly asked and aswered questions so that it has some konwledge of how i reply to those common requests and questions and can bring that knowledge to the drafts. As i tweak and send the drafts It would be

 great if claude could learn and get smarter overtime learning from my replies and those learnings coould compound overtime.

 Id assume a claude code + gmail mcp would be the way to go. i hear @gongrzhe/server-gmail-autoauth-mcp may be a good one to use.

 Thoughts?

 ⏺ Great idea — and you're closer to having this than you might think. Here are my thoughts:

 You already have Gmail MCP tools available in Claude Code — gmail_search_messages , gmail_read_message , gmail_read_thread , gmail_create_draft . That's the core read/write loop right there.

 You also already have a voice profile from /train_voice (24 months of Gmail analysis) sitting in memory/voice_profile.md . That's a head start on the "sound like Steven" piece.

 The "auto-draft when I open an email" trigger — that's the hard part. True auto-trigger would need a Chrome extension or Gmail add-on, which is a whole separate project. But the practical version that actually works:

 - You open Claude Code, say something like "draft replies for my unread emails"

 - I pull unread messages, read the threads for context, draft replies in your voice

 - Drafts appear right in Gmail — you review, tweak, send

 - Batch 10-20 emails in minutes instead of one at a time

 For the learning loop:

 1. Phase 1 — Mine your history. Analyze your sent mail for common Q&A patterns (pricing questions, meeting requests, follow-ups, etc.) and build a response playbook

 2. Phase 2 — Draft with context. Use voice profile + playbook + thread context to generate drafts

 3. Phase 3 — Learn from edits. Compare what I drafted vs. what you actually sent — store the delta as feedback that compounds over time

 On the MCP choice: The @gongrzhe/server-gmail-autoauth-mcp may offer more granular control, but let's first check what the built-in Gmail MCP can do — it might be sufficient and it's already connected. If it's missing something (like batch operations

 or label management), we add the other one.

 My recommendation: Start right now by analyzing your sent emails to build the response playbook and voice rules. That's the foundation everything else builds on. Then we test-drive it on a batch of real unread emails today.

 Want to start with the sent email analysis, or do you want to talk through the workflow more first?

 ⏺ Here's what I'm proposing:

 No new Python modules, no Scout changes, no Railway deploys. This is entirely a Claude Code workflow using the Gmail MCP tools you already have connected.

 5 phases:

 1. Build Response Playbook — Analyze your sent emails (last 12 months) to extract categorized Q&A patterns. Pricing questions, scheduling, follow-ups, technical, procurement, etc. with your real snippets and response structures. Saved to

 memory/response_playbook.md .

 2. Anti-AI-Tell Rules + Prompt Template — Add explicit banned patterns to your voice profile (em dashes, corporate phrases, consecutive "I" sentences). Create prompts/reply_draft.md as the core template.

 3. Document the Workflow — Add instructions to CLAUDE.md so any Claude Code session knows the flow: search unread → read threads → classify → draft → create threaded Gmail drafts.

 4. Test on Real Emails — Draft replies to 5 real unread emails, you review for voice accuracy and AI tells, we iterate.

 5. Learning Loop (future sessions) — "Learn from my sends" compares what I drafted vs what you actually sent. Corrections compound in the voice profile and playbook over time.

 Total new API costs: $0 — everything runs within Claude Code conversation + free Gmail MCP calls.

 The key unlock: gmail_create_draft supports threadId , so drafts appear as proper threaded replies in Gmail. You open your inbox, the drafts are already there waiting.

 ⎿  Tool use rejected

 ⏺ Let me actually think about this before rewriting.

 Read 2 files (ctrl+o to expand)

 ⏺ Good catch on something — the voice profile says Steven does use em dashes occasionally. The original plan banned them outright. That's wrong. Let me think through all the actual problems before rewriting.

 Problems I found with the original plan:

 1. No verification first. The plan builds a playbook before even confirming the Gmail MCP works end-to-end. If thread reads fail or drafts don't thread correctly, everything downstream is wasted work.

 2. Playbook building is vague at the critical step. "Read 150-200 reply threads" in a single Claude Code session means potentially millions of tokens of thread context. No plan for how to handle that. Will hit context limits.

 3. Missing email classification. Not every unread email needs a reply. Newsletters, CC'd threads, FYIs, notifications. And some emails NEED specific data (pricing, calendar, account info) that can't be faked. The plan treats all emails the same.

 4. Em dash ban is factually wrong. Steven's own voice profile says he uses them "occasionally for asides." Banning them entirely would make drafts LESS like Steven, not more.

 5. Draft quality degrades at scale. Processing 15-20 emails sequentially in one conversation fills context. By email #15, drafts get formulaic. No mitigation.

 6. Learning loop stores metadata but not draft text. Can't compare drafts to actual sends if you only logged thread_id and category.

 7. CLAUDE.md is already 500+ lines. Adding a full workflow section bloats every session's context, even non-email ones.

 ⏺ Updated plan

 ⎿  /plan to preview

 ⏺ Here's the rebuilt plan. Key changes from the original and why:

 1. Verification first (was buried in Phase 4). The entire plan depends on Gmail MCP's threadId drafting working correctly. If it doesn't, everything is wasted. Now it's Step 1.

 2. Two-pass playbook building (was vague "read 150-200 threads"). Reading 200 full threads in one session would blow context limits. Now it's: Pass 1 surveys subjects/snippets (lightweight), Pass 2 deep-dives 5-8 threads per category (focused,

 manageable). ~60-80 total thread reads, each analyzed independently.

 3. Email classification system (was missing entirely). Added DRAFT/FLAG/SKIP classification. "Send me a quote" can't be replied to with just voice matching — it gets flagged with [STEVEN: need X] inline. Newsletters get skipped. This prevents the

 system from generating wrong or empty replies.

 4. Em dash rule corrected (was factually wrong). Steven's own voice profile says he uses them "occasionally for asides." Changed from "ban completely" to "don't use as clause connectors like AI does."

 5. Batch size limits (was missing). Added "process in batches of 5-7" to prevent quality degradation from context accumulation. By email #15 in a single conversation, drafts get formulaic.

 6. Draft log stores actual text (was metadata-only). The learning loop can't compare drafts to sends if it only stored thread_id and category.

 7. CLAUDE.md section is 10 lines, not a full workflow (was going to bloat an already 500+ line file). Detailed rules live in prompts/reply_draft.md where they belong.

 ⏺ I've been sitting with the full picture. Here's what I'm actually changing and why, then the rebuilt plan.

 Foundational challenge I considered and rejected: Replacing the static playbook with dynamic few-shot retrieval (search Gmail for similar past replies at draft time). It's architecturally elegant but wrong for this problem. Steven's inbound

 categories are predictable — pricing, scheduling, follow-ups, technical, procurement. A static playbook covering 8-10 categories handles 90%+ of his inbox. Dynamic retrieval adds latency per email, matches might be poor, and it's more complex to

 execute. The playbook is correct.

 Structural issue I'm fixing: V2 puts voice rules in three places — voice_profile.md , the anti-AI-tell section added to voice_profile.md , AND prompts/reply_draft.md . That's fragmented. Voice rules belong in the voice profile. The prompt file should

 contain WORKFLOW instructions only (how to classify, draft, and output). Clean separation: voice profile = how Steven sounds. Playbook = what Steven says. Prompt file = how the drafting process works.

 Missing quality gate: V2's testing step has no pass/fail criteria. If the first 5 drafts are mostly bad, continuing to build the learning loop is wasted work. Need an explicit gate: if <3 of 5 are usable without significant rewriting, stop and

 iterate before proceeding.

 Missing scope boundaries: V2 doesn't define what emails are out of scope. Sales outreach TO Steven, recruiters, internal company threads, support tickets. The SKIP classification needs to be comprehensive.

 Missing CC handling: Business threads have CCs. Need a rule for reply-all behavior.

 ⎿  /plan to preview

 ⏺ What changed from V2 and why:

 1. Voice rules consolidated into voice_profile.md only. V2 had them in both the voice profile and the prompt file. The prompt file now contains only workflow instructions. Single source of truth — no drift, no maintenance burden.

 2. Explicit quality gate added at Step 5. If <3 of 5 drafts are usable, stop and iterate. V2 had no pass/fail criteria, which means you could build the learning loop on top of bad drafts and waste a session discovering it doesn't work.

 3. CC/reply-all handling. V2 ignored CCs entirely. Business threads always have them. Now there's a clear rule: include original CC list, strip automated addresses.

 4. Comprehensive SKIP classification. V2 said "newsletters, notifications." Now it covers: sales outreach TO Steven, recruiters, internal company threads, support tickets, CC-only threads, already-replied emails.

 5. Failure mode for Step 1. V2 said "if any step fails, stop." Now it says: if threadId drafting fails, fall back to non-threaded drafts with "Re:" subjects. If MCP can't create drafts at all, fall back to GAS bridge. Degrades gracefully instead of

 blocking.

 6. Draft log cleanup rule. V2 stored full draft text with no cleanup plan. Now: delete entries >14 days at the start of each session. Never exceeds ~50 entries.

 7. Removed template variable syntax from prompt file. V2 had {voice_profile} , {playbook_category} as if Claude fills in a template. Claude Code doesn't work that way — it reads files and reasons. The prompt file now says "Load these files into

 context" which is how it actually executes.
