 170 ## Drafting Rules
 171 - - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.
 171 + - **Default to pushing for a Zoom call before sending pricing.** Steven's first preference is always to get them on a call to discuss options and quote. If they push back or after already trying, send pricing in email. For internation
 + al leads, send pricing directly without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).
 172 - **NEVER fabricate or assume product features.** Only state things that are verified facts about CodeCombat. If unsure, use a frame ("We have a lot of options that might work for your students") rather than a specific claim. Getting

 caught making something up damages Steven's credibility.

 173 - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 on.

 174 - **Budget constraints:** Don't just dump free content immediately. First try to work with their budget, offer a discount, or suggest a pilot. Free content is the fallback, not the lead.

 167 >

 168 > *Through the end of September*, we will be offering a promo..."

 170 + ## Anti-AI-Tell Checklist

 171 + These are the most common patterns that make AI-drafted emails detectable. Violating any of these breaks the illusion:

 172 + - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 173 + - Semicolons. Steven never uses them.

 174 + - "I'd be happy to" / "I'd love to" / "Absolutely!" / "Great question!" / "That's a great point"

 175 + - "I hope this finds you well" / "I wanted to reach out" / "Just circling back" / "per our conversation"

 176 + - Corporate vocabulary: leverage, streamline, robust, comprehensive, cutting-edge, foster, delve, facilitate, utilize

 177 + - Starting 2+ consecutive sentences with "I"

 178 + - Three-part escalating lists ("fast, efficient, and powerful")

 179 + - Being more enthusiastic than the sender

 180 + - Overly parallel sentence structures

 181 + - Filler transitions: "Additionally," "Furthermore," "Moreover," "In terms of"

 182 + - Formal connectors: "However," "Nevertheless," "Consequently"

 183 + - Hedging phrases: "It's worth noting that," "It's important to mention"

 184 +

 185 ---

 187 **Summary**: Steven writes like a helpful human, not a sales robot. He's efficient, empathetic, and adapts fluidly to each recipient's communication style. His emails prioritize clarity and actionability over polish, and he's comforta

 ble being brief when brevity serves the conversation.

 188 No newline at end of file

 1. From: Kirk Ellern KEllern@washoeschools.net

 Subject: Re: [EXTERNAL] Kirk, coding at E Otis Vaughn Middle School

 Classification: DRAFT (Category: Budget Constraints)

 Inbound: "Hi Steve, I'm afraid your prices are way above our ability. Thanks Kirk"

 Draft:

 ▎ Hey Kirk,

 ▎ No problem at all. We actually have a ton of free content built into the platform that you and your students can start using right away:

 ▎ - The 1st unit in CodeCombat

 ▎ - The 1st chapter in Ozaria

 ▎ - AI League (educational Esports) - free with real prizes

 ▎ - CodeCombat Worlds via Roblox - free learning levels

 ▎ - CoCo Jr for K-5 block-based coding

 ▎ - Tons of Hour of Code activities

 ▎ We also have a grants site with pre-built templates and a hand-picked list of CS grants if that ever becomes useful: codecombat.com/grants

 ▎ Let me know if you want me to set you up with some free licenses to try things out. Happy to help whenever you're ready.

 ---

 2. From: Arlington ISD aisd@customer.ionwave.net

 Subject: Arlington Independent School District Bid Question Answered: 26-38 (Speech-Language Therapy Services)

 Classification: SKIP (automated bid notification, not relevant - Speech-Language Therapy, not CodeCombat)

 ---

 3. From: Juniper Gill gilljv@nv.ccsd.net

 Subject: Re: Wilbur & Theresa Faiss Middle School's CodeCombat request

 Classification: DRAFT (Category: Pricing / Product Info)

 Inbound: "I am no longer at Faiss MS. Although I am interested in a quote. It would be for approximately 75-100 student accounts. Also, what programs do you offer?"

 Draft:

 ▎ Hey Juniper,

 ▎ Got it, glad you're still interested!

 ▎ For 75-100 licenses, pricing would be $50 per student for an annual license. So 75 would be $3,750 and 100 would be $5,000.

 ▎ Here's what's included with your license:

 ▎ - Code Combat - text-based coding in Python and Java Script (grades 6-12)

 ▎ - Ozaria - narrative-driven CS fundamentals (grades 6-10)

 ▎ - CoCo Jr - block-based coding for K-5

 ▎ - AI HackStack - AI/prompt engineering curriculum

 ▎ - AI Algebra - our newest course, launched this March

 ▎ - Cybersecurity - coming this fall

 ▎ - AI League - educational Esports tournaments with real prizes

 ▎ All of that comes with the teacher dashboard, progress tracking, and a full library of teacher resources. No CS background needed.

 ▎ What school are you at now? Want to hop on a quick call so I can walk you through it? Pick any time here: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 ---

 4. From: Alice Edwards edwarav@nv.ccsd.net

 Subject: Re: Rancho High School's CodeCombat request

 Classification: DRAFT (Category: Free Trial / Pilot)

 Inbound: "Trial was too short, didn't move forward. Have a summer ESY program starting June 11, only a month long. Want to test with disabled students as a benchmark."

 ▎ Totally understand on the timing. A month is actually plenty of time to get a solid feel for it.

 ▎ I can set you up with free licenses for your summer ESY program starting June 11. Just let me know how many students you're expecting and I'll get those ready for you ahead of time.

 ▎ We also have a lot of accessibility features that work well for diverse learners. Happy to walk you through those too if that would be helpful.

 ---

 5. From: Stephanie Boring stephanie.boring@gracetyler.org

 Classification: FLAG (Category: Free Trial - needs Steven to grant access)

 ▎ Let me know if you run into any issues or need anything else.

 ---

 ❯ ok here would be my changes to the drafts "1. Kirk Ellern (Budget Constraints) — REDRAFT:

 ▎ Hey Kirk,

 ▎ No problem at all. What does your budget look like? Are you needing it to be under some specific amount for this purchase or are you trying to get it down to a specific price per student?

 We work with schools in all kinds of budget situations and can almost always find something that fits!

 ▎ We also offer pilots and discounted options depending on the size and timing.

 And if budget is completely locked right now, we have a TON of free content your students can start using today and a grant writing specialist to help you plan for next semester.

 Either way - don’t give up yet!

 ▎ Pick a time here - we’ll go over all the options and i can even set you up with trial licenses for a while: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 ---

 3. Juniper Gill (Pricing / Product Info) — REDRAFT:

 ▎ Hey Juniper,

 ▎ Got it, glad you're still interested! No worries about the school change.

 ▎ For 75-100 students, we have a few different options depending on how you’re set up and what your use case looks like.

 ▎ Let’s connect via a zoom call whenever is convenient for you so I can walk you through everything and get you a fresh quote?

 Pick any time here: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 ---

 4. Alice Edwards (Free Trial / Pilot) — REDRAFT:

 ▎ Hey Alice,

 ▎ Got it, sorry the timing didn't work out last time. I can definitely set you up with free licenses for a little while. Can you tell me more about your summer ESY program starting June 11?

 How long will it run?

 How many students?

 What grades?

 Did you have any subjects or products in mind already that you wanted to use?

 ▎ Want to connect via Zoom before then so I can walk you through the best setup for your program? Pick any time here: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 ---

 5. Stephanie Boring (Unlock Content) — REDRAFT:

 ▎ Hey Stephanie,

 ▎ I went ahead and unlocked some more content for you. You should be good to go past level 9 now!

 How many students do you have?

 ▎ [STEVEN: Confirm you unlocked access for her before sending. If you also gave her 32 free licenses, mention that here.]

 ⎿   Added 12 lines
 189 - Formal connectors: "However," "Nevertheless," "Consequently"
 190 - Hedging phrases: "It's worth noting that," "It's important to mention"
 192 + ## Learned Corrections (from draft review 2026-04-07)
 193 + - **Ask specific budget questions, not generic.** "What does your budget look like?" is too vague. Better: "Are you needing it to be under some specific amount for this purchase or are you trying to get it down to a specific price per
 + student?"
 194 + - **Add encouragement for budget-constrained prospects.** "Don't give up yet!" — Steven is optimistic and solution-oriented, not just accommodating.

 195 + - **Mention the grant writing specialist** when budget is the issue. Not just free content — active help finding money.

 196 + - **Frame CTAs with incentives.** Not just "let's chat" — "we'll go over all the options and I can even set you up with trial licenses."

 197 + - **Use "Let's connect via a zoom call whenever is convenient"** — warmer and less transactional than "Want to hop on a quick call?"

 198 + - **Keep free license offers flexible.** Say "for a little while" not a specific timeframe in the initial reply. Lock down details later.

 199 + - **Ask scoping questions on separate lines.** How long? How many students? What grades? Each gets its own line for readability.

 200 + - **Acknowledge time gaps.** "A lot has likely changed since we last connected" — validates the prospect's return and creates curiosity.

 201 + - **Always add a follow-up question that opens the door.** Even simple ones like "How many students do you have?" keep the conversation going.

 202 + - **Never fabricate product features.** If unsure about a capability, don't mention it. Use frames instead.

 203 +

 204 ---

 206 **Summary**: Steven writes like a helpful human, not a sales robot. He's efficient, empathetic, and adapts fluidly to each recipient's communication style. His emails prioritize clarity and actionability over polish, and he's comforta

 ble being brief when brevity serves the conversation.

 207 No newline at end of file

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 15 lines

 167 >

 168 > *Through the end of September*, we will be offering a promo..."

 170 + ## Anti-AI-Tell Checklist

 171 + These are the most common patterns that make AI-drafted emails detectable. Violating any of these breaks the illusion:

 172 + - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 173 + - Semicolons. Steven never uses them.

 174 + - "I'd be happy to" / "I'd love to" / "Absolutely!" / "Great question!" / "That's a great point"

 175 + - "I hope this finds you well" / "I wanted to reach out" / "Just circling back" / "per our conversation"

 176 + - Corporate vocabulary: leverage, streamline, robust, comprehensive, cutting-edge, foster, delve, facilitate, utilize

 177 + - Starting 2+ consecutive sentences with "I"

 178 + - Three-part escalating lists ("fast, efficient, and powerful")

 179 + - Being more enthusiastic than the sender

 180 + - Overly parallel sentence structures

 181 + - Filler transitions: "Additionally," "Furthermore," "Moreover," "In terms of"

 182 + - Formal connectors: "However," "Nevertheless," "Consequently"

 183 + - Hedging phrases: "It's worth noting that," "It's important to mention"

 184 +

 185 ---

 187 **Summary**: Steven writes like a helpful human, not a sales robot. He's efficient, empathetic, and adapts fluidly to each recipient's communication style. His emails prioritize clarity and actionability over polish, and he's comforta

 ble being brief when brevity serves the conversation.

 188 No newline at end of file

 ⏺ Write ( prompts/reply_draft.md )

 ⎿   Wrote 96 lines to prompts/reply_draft.md

 1 # Email Reply Drafting — Workflow Instructions

 3 ## When to run

 4 Steven says "draft my emails", "check my inbox", "draft replies", or similar.

 6 ## Before starting

 ⏺ Really valuable feedback. Let me capture these learnings and redraft.

 Key corrections:

 - Never send pricing in email — always drive toward a call to discuss options and quote

 - Budget constraints — try to work with their budget / offer discount / pilot first, THEN mention free stuff

 - Don't fabricate product claims — only state verified facts. Frames are OK, fabricated features are not

 - Unlock content — Steven can unlock more free content + give ~30 days free licenses strategically

 - Tone — never risk sounding combative or dismissive, especially when someone expressed frustration

 6 ## 1. Pricing / Licensing Questions

 7 - **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, per-license, bulk pricing, quote, PO amount

 8 - **Steven's pattern:** Direct price answer first line → pricing breakdown → mention free content → soft CTA

 7 + **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, per-license, bulk pricing, quote, how licensing works

 8 + **Steven's pattern:** Almost NEVER sends pricing in email. Instead → acknowledge their interest → give a high-level sense of what's included → drive toward a call to discuss options, answer questions, and get them a fresh quote.

 9 **Real snippets:**

 10 - - "Our minimum purchase order is 10 annual licenses for $70 each ($700)."

 11 - - "For 120 licenses, the cost would be $50 x 120 licenses for 1 year = $6,000."

 12 - - "For 20 licenses, the cost would be as follows. $50 x 20 licenses for 1 year = $1000."

 10 + - "Want to hop on a quick call so I can walk you through everything and get you a fresh quote?"

 11 + - "I can go over all the options and pricing on a call. Pick any time here: [calendar link]"

 12 - "You have 3 options: the first two come with the teacher dashboard, progress tracking, and a plethora of teacher and student resources."

 14 - - "We also have a second option for you, which is a Self-Serve Purchase."

 15 - **Typical length:** Medium-long. Uses bullet lists and tables for pricing tiers. Always ends with scheduling link or soft CTA.

 16 - **Key detail:** Always mention the minimum is 10 licenses ($700) and that self-serve is an option for smaller orders.

 13 + **Typical length:** Medium. Focus on what's INCLUDED (the value), not specific dollar amounts. Always end with scheduling link.

 14 + **Key rule:** Do NOT include specific pricing numbers in email replies. Push for a call where Steven can discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 16 ## 2. Budget Constraints / Can't Afford

 17 **Trigger:** "Too expensive", "above our ability", "no budget", "can't spend"

 16 ## 2. Budget Constraints / Can't Afford

 17 **Trigger:** "Too expensive", "above our ability", "no budget", "can't spend"

 18 - **Steven's pattern:** Validate ("No problem at all") → offer free alternatives → list free content → mention grants → leave door open

 18 + **Steven's pattern:** Validate ("No problem at all") → first try to work with their budget or offer a discount/pilot → THEN mention free content and grants → leave door open. Don't just dump free stuff immediately — explore what might

 + work for them first.

 19 **Real snippets:**

 20 - "No problem at all. I'd be more than happy to give you some free licenses for 30 days if you'd like to dig in and explore it yourself or with your classes?"

 21 - "We also have tons of free stuff already built-in like: [bulleted list of free content]"

 66 **Calendar link:** https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 68 ## 6. Free Trial / Pilot Offers

 69 - **Trigger:** Prospect wants to try before buying, exploring options

 70 - **Steven's pattern:** Offer free licenses directly → specify duration → mention free content

 69 + **Trigger:** Prospect wants to try before buying, exploring options , asking for access past free levels

 70 + **Steven's pattern:** Offer free licenses directly → specify duration → mention free content. Steven can unlock additional free content and give ~30 days of free licenses to anyone at his discretion. He uses this strategically — when i

 + t will benefit the relationship or move the deal forward.

 71 **Real snippets:**

 72 - "I went ahead and granted you 125 licenses that will be active through the end of April."

 73 - "Sweet, I unlocked it, but also gave you 30 full licenses through April 18th."

 74 - "I'd be more than happy to give you some free licenses for 30 days if you'd like to dig in and explore it yourself or with your classes?"

 75 - "Since this is your first year using us, we will meet you wherever you are and offer you a pilot."

 76 **Typical length:** Short-medium. Direct action (already granted licenses) or concrete offer. No lengthy explanation of why pilots are great.

 77 + **Key detail:** Some content can be unlocked with a quick sales call, but Steven often just unlocks it if they ask via email. It just opens up a bit more free content.

 79 ## 7. No-Pressure Follow-up / Patience

 80 **Trigger:** Prospect is busy, delayed, or needs time

 167 >

 168 > *Through the end of September*, we will be offering a promo..."

 170 + ## Drafting Rules

 171 + - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.

 172 + - **NEVER fabricate or assume product features.** Only state things that are verified facts about CodeCombat. If unsure, use a frame ("We have a lot of options that might work for your students") rather than a specific claim. Getting

 + caught making something up damages Steven's credibility.

 173 + - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 + on.

 174 + - **Budget constraints:** Don't just dump free content immediately. First try to work with their budget, offer a discount, or suggest a pilot. Free content is the fallback, not the lead.

 175 + - **Drive toward calls:** Steven's strength is on the phone. Email is for opening doors and quick confirmations. Push toward scheduling a call whenever there's a real opportunity.

 176 +

 177 ## Anti-AI-Tell Checklist

 178 These are the most common patterns that make AI-drafted emails detectable. Violating any of these breaks the illusion:

 179 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 6 ## 1. Pricing / Licensing Questions

 7 **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, per-license, bulk pricing, quote, how licensing works

 8 - **Steven's pattern:** Almost NEVER sends pricing in email. Instead → acknowledge their interest → give a high-level sense of what's included → drive toward a call to discuss options, answer questions, and get them a fresh quote.

 8 + **Steven's pattern:** First preference is always to push for a Zoom call to discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. If they push back on a call or after already trying, Steven will send pricing in email. For internationa

 + l leads/contacts, send pricing directly in the email without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).

 9 **Real snippets:**

 10 - "Want to hop on a quick call so I can walk you through everything and get you a fresh quote?"

 11 - "I can go over all the options and pricing on a call. Pick any time here: [calendar link]"

 12 - "You have 3 options: the first two come with the teacher dashboard, progress tracking, and a plethora of teacher and student resources."

 13 - **Typical length:** Medium. Focus on what's INCLUDED (the value), not specific dollar amounts. Always end with scheduling link.

 14 - **Key rule:** Do NOT include specific pricing numbers in email replies. Push for a call where Steven can discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 13 + - "Our minimum purchase order is 10 annual licenses for $70 each ($700)."

 14 + - "For 20 licenses, the cost would be as follows. $50 x 20 licenses for 1 year = $1000."

 15 + **Typical length:** Medium. When pushing for a call, focus on what's INCLUDED (the value) and end with scheduling link. When sending pricing (after call attempt or international), use bullet lists and tables.

 16 + **Key rule:** Default to pushing for a Zoom call first. Send pricing in email only after trying for a call, if they push back, or for international leads. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 170 ## Drafting Rules

 171 - - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.

 171 + - **Default to pushing for a Zoom call before sending pricing.** Steven's first preference is always to get them on a call to discuss options and quote. If they push back or after already trying, send pricing in email. For internation

 + al leads, send pricing directly without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).

 172 - **NEVER fabricate or assume product features.** Only state things that are verified facts about CodeCombat. If unsure, use a frame ("We have a lot of options that might work for your students") rather than a specific claim. Getting

 caught making something up damages Steven's credibility.

 173 - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 on.

 174 - **Budget constraints:** Don't just dump free content immediately. First try to work with their budget, offer a discount, or suggest a pilot. Free content is the fallback, not the lead.

 ⏺ Fixed. The nuance is:

 - Domestic, first touch: Push for Zoom call, don't lead with pricing

 - Domestic, after trying / they push back: Send pricing in email

 - International: Send pricing directly (unless significant opp)

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 12 lines

 189 - Formal connectors: "However," "Nevertheless," "Consequently"

 190 - Hedging phrases: "It's worth noting that," "It's important to mention"

 192 + ## Learned Corrections (from draft review 2026-04-07)

 193 + - **Ask specific budget questions, not generic.** "What does your budget look like?" is too vague. Better: "Are you needing it to be under some specific amount for this purchase or are you trying to get it down to a specific price per

 + student?"

 194 + - **Add encouragement for budget-constrained prospects.** "Don't give up yet!" — Steven is optimistic and solution-oriented, not just accommodating.

 195 + - **Mention the grant writing specialist** when budget is the issue. Not just free content — active help finding money.

 196 + - **Frame CTAs with incentives.** Not just "let's chat" — "we'll go over all the options and I can even set you up with trial licenses."

 197 + - **Use "Let's connect via a zoom call whenever is convenient"** — warmer and less transactional than "Want to hop on a quick call?"

 198 + - **Keep free license offers flexible.** Say "for a little while" not a specific timeframe in the initial reply. Lock down details later.

 199 + - **Ask scoping questions on separate lines.** How long? How many students? What grades? Each gets its own line for readability.

 200 + - **Acknowledge time gaps.** "A lot has likely changed since we last connected" — validates the prospect's return and creates curiosity.

 201 + - **Always add a follow-up question that opens the door.** Even simple ones like "How many students do you have?" keep the conversation going.

 202 + - **Never fabricate product features.** If unsure about a capability, don't mention it. Use frames instead.

 203 +

 204 ---

 206 **Summary**: Steven writes like a helpful human, not a sales robot. He's efficient, empathetic, and adapts fluidly to each recipient's communication style. His emails prioritize clarity and actionability over polish, and he's comforta

 ble being brief when brevity serves the conversation.

 207 No newline at end of file

 ⏺ Really valuable feedback. Let me capture these learnings and redraft.

 Key corrections:

 - Never send pricing in email — always drive toward a call to discuss options and quote

 - Budget constraints — try to work with their budget / offer discount / pilot first, THEN mention free stuff

 - Don't fabricate product claims — only state verified facts. Frames are OK, fabricated features are not

 - Unlock content — Steven can unlock more free content + give ~30 days free licenses strategically

 - Tone — never risk sounding combative or dismissive, especially when someone expressed frustration

 6 ## 1. Pricing / Licensing Questions

 7 - **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, per-license, bulk pricing, quote, PO amount

 8 - **Steven's pattern:** Direct price answer first line → pricing breakdown → mention free content → soft CTA

 7 + **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, per-license, bulk pricing, quote, how licensing works

 8 + **Steven's pattern:** Almost NEVER sends pricing in email. Instead → acknowledge their interest → give a high-level sense of what's included → drive toward a call to discuss options, answer questions, and get them a fresh quote.

 9 **Real snippets:**

 10 - - "Our minimum purchase order is 10 annual licenses for $70 each ($700)."

 11 - - "For 120 licenses, the cost would be $50 x 120 licenses for 1 year = $6,000."

 12 - - "For 20 licenses, the cost would be as follows. $50 x 20 licenses for 1 year = $1000."

 10 + - "Want to hop on a quick call so I can walk you through everything and get you a fresh quote?"

 11 + - "I can go over all the options and pricing on a call. Pick any time here: [calendar link]"

 12 - "You have 3 options: the first two come with the teacher dashboard, progress tracking, and a plethora of teacher and student resources."

 14 - - "We also have a second option for you, which is a Self-Serve Purchase."

 15 - **Typical length:** Medium-long. Uses bullet lists and tables for pricing tiers. Always ends with scheduling link or soft CTA.

 16 - **Key detail:** Always mention the minimum is 10 licenses ($700) and that self-serve is an option for smaller orders.

 13 + **Typical length:** Medium. Focus on what's INCLUDED (the value), not specific dollar amounts. Always end with scheduling link.

 14 + **Key rule:** Do NOT include specific pricing numbers in email replies. Push for a call where Steven can discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 16 ## 2. Budget Constraints / Can't Afford

 17 **Trigger:** "Too expensive", "above our ability", "no budget", "can't spend"

 16 ## 2. Budget Constraints / Can't Afford

 17 **Trigger:** "Too expensive", "above our ability", "no budget", "can't spend"

 18 - **Steven's pattern:** Validate ("No problem at all") → offer free alternatives → list free content → mention grants → leave door open

 18 + **Steven's pattern:** Validate ("No problem at all") → first try to work with their budget or offer a discount/pilot → THEN mention free content and grants → leave door open. Don't just dump free stuff immediately — explore what might

 + work for them first.

 68 ## 6. Free Trial / Pilot Offers

 69 - **Trigger:** Prospect wants to try before buying, exploring options

 70 - **Steven's pattern:** Offer free licenses directly → specify duration → mention free content

 69 + **Trigger:** Prospect wants to try before buying, exploring options , asking for access past free levels

 70 + **Steven's pattern:** Offer free licenses directly → specify duration → mention free content. Steven can unlock additional free content and give ~30 days of free licenses to anyone at his discretion. He uses this strategically — when i

 + t will benefit the relationship or move the deal forward.

 71 **Real snippets:**

 72 - "I went ahead and granted you 125 licenses that will be active through the end of April."

 73 - "Sweet, I unlocked it, but also gave you 30 full licenses through April 18th."

 74 - "I'd be more than happy to give you some free licenses for 30 days if you'd like to dig in and explore it yourself or with your classes?"

 75 - "Since this is your first year using us, we will meet you wherever you are and offer you a pilot."

 76 **Typical length:** Short-medium. Direct action (already granted licenses) or concrete offer. No lengthy explanation of why pilots are great.

 79 ## 7. No-Pressure Follow-up / Patience

 80 **Trigger:** Prospect is busy, delayed, or needs time

 167 >

 168 > *Through the end of September*, we will be offering a promo..."

 170 + ## Drafting Rules

 171 + - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.

 172 + - **NEVER fabricate or assume product features.** Only state things that are verified facts about CodeCombat. If unsure, use a frame ("We have a lot of options that might work for your students") rather than a specific claim. Getting

 + caught making something up damages Steven's credibility.

 173 + - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 + on.

 174 + - **Budget constraints:** Don't just dump free content immediately. First try to work with their budget, offer a discount, or suggest a pilot. Free content is the fallback, not the lead.

 175 + - **Drive toward calls:** Steven's strength is on the phone. Email is for opening doors and quick confirmations. Push toward scheduling a call whenever there's a real opportunity.

 176 +

 177 ## Anti-AI-Tell Checklist

 178 These are the most common patterns that make AI-drafted emails detectable. Violating any of these breaks the illusion:

 179 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 + l leads/contacts, send pricing directly in the email without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).

 9 **Real snippets:**

 11 - "I can go over all the options and pricing on a call. Pick any time here: [calendar link]"

 170 ## Drafting Rules

 171 - - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.

 171 + - **Default to pushing for a Zoom call before sending pricing.** Steven's first preference is always to get them on a call to discuss options and quote. If they push back or after already trying, send pricing in email. For internation

 + al leads, send pricing directly without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).

 172 - **NEVER fabricate or assume product features.** Only state things that are verified facts about CodeCombat. If unsure, use a frame ("We have a lot of options that might work for your students") rather than a specific claim. Getting

 caught making something up damages Steven's credibility.

 173 - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 on.

 174 - **Budget constraints:** Don't just dump free content immediately. First try to work with their budget, offer a discount, or suggest a pilot. Free content is the fallback, not the lead.

 ⏺ Fixed. The nuance is:

 - Domestic, first touch: Push for Zoom call, don't lead with pricing

 - Domestic, after trying / they push back: Send pricing in email

 - International: Send pricing directly (unless significant opp)

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 12 lines

 189 - Formal connectors: "However," "Nevertheless," "Consequently"

 190 - Hedging phrases: "It's worth noting that," "It's important to mention"

 192 + ## Learned Corrections (from draft review 2026-04-07)

 193 + - **Ask specific budget questions, not generic.** "What does your budget look like?" is too vague. Better: "Are you needing it to be under some specific amount for this purchase or are you trying to get it down to a specific price per

 + student?"

 194 + - **Add encouragement for budget-constrained prospects.** "Don't give up yet!" — Steven is optimistic and solution-oriented, not just accommodating.

 195 + - **Mention the grant writing specialist** when budget is the issue. Not just free content — active help finding money.

 196 + - **Frame CTAs with incentives.** Not just "let's chat" — "we'll go over all the options and I can even set you up with trial licenses."

 197 + - **Use "Let's connect via a zoom call whenever is convenient"** — warmer and less transactional than "Want to hop on a quick call?"

 198 + - **Keep free license offers flexible.** Say "for a little while" not a specific timeframe in the initial reply. Lock down details later.

 199 + - **Ask scoping questions on separate lines.** How long? How many students? What grades? Each gets its own line for readability.

 200 + - **Acknowledge time gaps.** "A lot has likely changed since we last connected" — validates the prospect's return and creates curiosity.

 201 + - **Always add a follow-up question that opens the door.** Even simple ones like "How many students do you have?" keep the conversation going.

 202 + - **Never fabricate product features.** If unsure about a capability, don't mention it. Use frames instead.

 203 +

 204 ---

 206 **Summary**: Steven writes like a helpful human, not a sales robot. He's efficient, empathetic, and adapts fluidly to each recipient's communication style. His emails prioritize clarity and actionability over polish, and he's comforta

 ble being brief when brevity serves the conversation.

 ---

 1. From: Kirk Ellern KEllern@washoeschools.net

 Subject: Re: [EXTERNAL] Kirk, coding at E Otis Vaughn Middle School

 Classification: DRAFT (Category: Budget Constraints)

 Inbound: "Hi Steve, I'm afraid your prices are way above our ability. Thanks Kirk"

 Draft:

 ▎ Hey Kirk,

 ▎ No problem at all. We actually have a ton of free content built into the platform that you and your students can start using right away:

 ▎ - The 1st unit in CodeCombat

 ▎ - The 1st chapter in Ozaria

 ▎ - AI League (educational Esports) - free with real prizes

 ▎ - CodeCombat Worlds via Roblox - free learning levels

 ▎ - CoCo Jr for K-5 block-based coding

 ▎ - Tons of Hour of Code activities

 ▎ We also have a grants site with pre-built templates and a hand-picked list of CS grants if that ever becomes useful: codecombat.com/grants

 ▎ Let me know if you want me to set you up with some free licenses to try things out. Happy to help whenever you're ready.

 ---

 2. From: Arlington ISD aisd@customer.ionwave.net

 Subject: Arlington Independent School District Bid Question Answered: 26-38 (Speech-Language Therapy Services)

 Classification: SKIP (automated bid notification, not relevant - Speech-Language Therapy, not CodeCombat)

 ---

 3. From: Juniper Gill gilljv@nv.ccsd.net

 Subject: Re: Wilbur & Theresa Faiss Middle School's CodeCombat request

 Classification: DRAFT (Category: Pricing / Product Info)

 Inbound: "I am no longer at Faiss MS. Although I am interested in a quote. It would be for approximately 75-100 student accounts. Also, what programs do you offer?"

 Draft:

 ▎ Hey Juniper,

 ▎ Got it, glad you're still interested!

 ▎ For 75-100 licenses, pricing would be $50 per student for an annual license. So 75 would be $3,750 and 100 would be $5,000.

 ▎ Here's what's included with your license:

 ▎ - Code Combat - text-based coding in Python and Java Script (grades 6-12)

 ▎ - Ozaria - narrative-driven CS fundamentals (grades 6-10)

 ▎ - CoCo Jr - block-based coding for K-5

 ▎ - AI HackStack - AI/prompt engineering curriculum

 ▎ - AI Algebra - our newest course, launched this March

 ▎ - Cybersecurity - coming this fall

 ▎ - AI League - educational Esports tournaments with real prizes

 ▎ All of that comes with the teacher dashboard, progress tracking, and a full library of teacher resources. No CS background needed.

 ▎ What school are you at now? Want to hop on a quick call so I can walk you through it? Pick any time here: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 ---

 4. From: Alice Edwards edwarav@nv.ccsd.net

 Subject: Re: Rancho High School's CodeCombat request

 Classification: DRAFT (Category: Free Trial / Pilot)

 Inbound: "Trial was too short, didn't move forward. Have a summer ESY program starting June 11, only a month long. Want to test with disabled students as a benchmark."

 Draft:

 ▎ Hey Alice,

 ▎ Totally understand on the timing. A month is actually plenty of time to get a solid feel for it.

 ▎ I can set you up with free licenses for your summer ESY program starting June 11. Just let me know how many students you're expecting and I'll get those ready for you ahead of time.

 ▎ We also have a lot of accessibility features that work well for diverse learners. Happy to walk you through those too if that would be helpful.

 ---

 5. From: Stephanie Boring stephanie.boring@gracetyler.org

 Subject: CodeCombat CS1 Curriculum

 Classification: FLAG (Category: Free Trial - needs Steven to grant access)

 Inbound: "Is there any way we can be allowed access for the course past level 9? I have 32 students."

 Draft:

 ▎ Hey Stephanie,

 ▎ [STEVEN: Can you unlock access past level 9 / grant 32 free licenses for her? If yes, update this draft with what you did. If this needs a paid license, adjust accordingly.]

 ▎ I went ahead and [unlocked access / gave you 32 licenses through the end of the school year]. You should be all set now!

 ▎ Let me know if you run into any issues or need anything else.

 ---

 That's 3 DRAFTs, 1 SKIP, and 1 FLAG. How do these look? Check them against your four criteria:

 1. Voice — Do they sound like you?

 2. AI tells — Any phrases or patterns that smell like AI?

 3. Content — Right info, right category?

 4. Energy — Right length and tone for each email?

 ❯ 1. the voice sounds like me. 2. No AI tells. 3. the content is somewhat close but i almost never send pricing in an ameail and the pricing you sent is old (regarding the 3rd test draft above). I would try to incetivize and encourage them to pick

 out a tiem to meet with me so taht we can discuss everything and so i can answer any questions and go over all the options and get them a fresh quote. Regarding test draft 1 i would try to see if we could do something to work with thier budget or

 offer a discount or a pilot first before just sending free stuff or send the free stuff with those other attempts. Regarding test draft 4, i think you made some assumptions about the accessibility features. make sure you actually know things for fact

 and dont make assumptions that can casue issues for me when i am asked about it or need to respond to it. Its ok to use frames though (like in the email sequence rules). FOr test draft 5. We allow some content to be unlocked with a quick sales call

 but oftentimes i just will unlock it for them if they ask via email. IT just unlocks alittle more free content. I also have the ability to give about 30 days of free licenses to anone at my discretion. I try to use this in ways that actually will

 benefit me.

 4. the length and tne is pretty good. One note that i saw was in test draft 4 wher you wrote "A month is actually plenty of time to get a solid feel for it." in response to her saying "Trial was too short, didn't move forward. Have a summer ESY

 program starting June 11, only a month long. Want to test with disabled students as a benchmark." i dont ever want this to have a chance of coming off as combative so we need to be careful with our wording and phrasing.

 ⏺ Really valuable feedback. Let me capture these learnings and redraft.

 Key corrections:

 - Never send pricing in email — always drive toward a call to discuss options and quote

 - Budget constraints — try to work with their budget / offer discount / pilot first, THEN mention free stuff

 - Don't fabricate product claims — only state verified facts. Frames are OK, fabricated features are not

 - Unlock content — Steven can unlock more free content + give ~30 days free licenses strategically

 - Tone — never risk sounding combative or dismissive, especially when someone expressed frustration

 Let me update the playbook and voice profile with these, then redraft.

 ⏺ Update ( memory/response_playbook.md )

 ⎿   Added 6 lines, removed 8 lines

 4 ---

 6 ## 1. Pricing / Licensing Questions

 7 - **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, per-license, bulk pricing, quote, PO amount

 8 - **Steven's pattern:** Direct price answer first line → pricing breakdown → mention free content → soft CTA

 7 + **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, per-license, bulk pricing, quote, how licensing works

 8 + **Steven's pattern:** Almost NEVER sends pricing in email. Instead → acknowledge their interest → give a high-level sense of what's included → drive toward a call to discuss options, answer questions, and get them a fresh quote.

 9 **Real snippets:**

 10 - - "Our minimum purchase order is 10 annual licenses for $70 each ($700)."

 11 - - "For 120 licenses, the cost would be $50 x 120 licenses for 1 year = $6,000."

 12 - - "For 20 licenses, the cost would be as follows. $50 x 20 licenses for 1 year = $1000."

 10 + - "Want to hop on a quick call so I can walk you through everything and get you a fresh quote?"

 11 + - "I can go over all the options and pricing on a call. Pick any time here: [calendar link]"

 12 - "You have 3 options: the first two come with the teacher dashboard, progress tracking, and a plethora of teacher and student resources."

 14 - - "We also have a second option for you, which is a Self-Serve Purchase."

 15 - **Typical length:** Medium-long. Uses bullet lists and tables for pricing tiers. Always ends with scheduling link or soft CTA.

 16 - **Key detail:** Always mention the minimum is 10 licenses ($700) and that self-serve is an option for smaller orders.

 13 + **Typical length:** Medium. Focus on what's INCLUDED (the value), not specific dollar amounts. Always end with scheduling link.

 14 + **Key rule:** Do NOT include specific pricing numbers in email replies. Push for a call where Steven can discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 16 ## 2. Budget Constraints / Can't Afford

 17 **Trigger:** "Too expensive", "above our ability", "no budget", "can't spend"

 ⏺ Update ( memory/response_playbook.md )

 ⎿   Added 1 line, removed 1 line

 16 ## 2. Budget Constraints / Can't Afford

 17 **Trigger:** "Too expensive", "above our ability", "no budget", "can't spend"

 18 - **Steven's pattern:** Validate ("No problem at all") → offer free alternatives → list free content → mention grants → leave door open

 18 + **Steven's pattern:** Validate ("No problem at all") → first try to work with their budget or offer a discount/pilot → THEN mention free content and grants → leave door open. Don't just dump free stuff immediately — explore what might

 + work for them first.

 19 **Real snippets:**

 20 - "No problem at all. I'd be more than happy to give you some free licenses for 30 days if you'd like to dig in and explore it yourself or with your classes?"

 21 - "We also have tons of free stuff already built-in like: [bulleted list of free content]"

 66 **Calendar link:** https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 68 ## 6. Free Trial / Pilot Offers

 69 - **Trigger:** Prospect wants to try before buying, exploring options

 70 - **Steven's pattern:** Offer free licenses directly → specify duration → mention free content

 69 + **Trigger:** Prospect wants to try before buying, exploring options , asking for access past free levels

 70 + **Steven's pattern:** Offer free licenses directly → specify duration → mention free content. Steven can unlock additional free content and give ~30 days of free licenses to anyone at his discretion. He uses this strategically — when i

 + t will benefit the relationship or move the deal forward.

 71 **Real snippets:**

 72 - "I went ahead and granted you 125 licenses that will be active through the end of April."

 73 - "Sweet, I unlocked it, but also gave you 30 full licenses through April 18th."

 74 - "I'd be more than happy to give you some free licenses for 30 days if you'd like to dig in and explore it yourself or with your classes?"

 75 - "Since this is your first year using us, we will meet you wherever you are and offer you a pilot."

 76 **Typical length:** Short-medium. Direct action (already granted licenses) or concrete offer. No lengthy explanation of why pilots are great.

 77 + **Key detail:** Some content can be unlocked with a quick sales call, but Steven often just unlocks it if they ask via email. It just opens up a bit more free content.

 79 ## 7. No-Pressure Follow-up / Patience

 80 **Trigger:** Prospect is busy, delayed, or needs time

 ⏺ Now let me add the key learnings to the voice profile.

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 7 lines

 167 >

 168 > *Through the end of September*, we will be offering a promo..."

 170 + ## Drafting Rules

 171 + - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.

 172 + - **NEVER fabricate or assume product features.** Only state things that are verified facts about CodeCombat. If unsure, use a frame ("We have a lot of options that might work for your students") rather than a specific claim. Getting

 + caught making something up damages Steven's credibility.

 173 + - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 + on.

 174 + - **Budget constraints:** Don't just dump free content immediately. First try to work with their budget, offer a discount, or suggest a pilot. Free content is the fallback, not the lead.

 175 + - **Drive toward calls:** Steven's strength is on the phone. Email is for opening doors and quick confirmations. Push toward scheduling a call whenever there's a real opportunity.

 176 +

 177 ## Anti-AI-Tell Checklist

 178 These are the most common patterns that make AI-drafted emails detectable. Violating any of these breaks the illusion:

 179 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 ⎿   Added 5 lines, removed 3 lines

 6 ## 1. Pricing / Licensing Questions

 7 **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, per-license, bulk pricing, quote, how licensing works

 8 - **Steven's pattern:** Almost NEVER sends pricing in email. Instead → acknowledge their interest → give a high-level sense of what's included → drive toward a call to discuss options, answer questions, and get them a fresh quote.

 8 + **Steven's pattern:** First preference is always to push for a Zoom call to discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. If they push back on a call or after already trying, Steven will send pricing in email. For internationa

 + l leads/contacts, send pricing directly in the email without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).

 9 **Real snippets:**

 10 - "Want to hop on a quick call so I can walk you through everything and get you a fresh quote?"

 11 - "I can go over all the options and pricing on a call. Pick any time here: [calendar link]"

 12 - "You have 3 options: the first two come with the teacher dashboard, progress tracking, and a plethora of teacher and student resources."

 13 - **Typical length:** Medium. Focus on what's INCLUDED (the value), not specific dollar amounts. Always end with scheduling link.

 14 - **Key rule:** Do NOT include specific pricing numbers in email replies. Push for a call where Steven can discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 13 + - "Our minimum purchase order is 10 annual licenses for $70 each ($700)."

 14 + - "For 20 licenses, the cost would be as follows. $50 x 20 licenses for 1 year = $1000."

 15 + **Typical length:** Medium. When pushing for a call, focus on what's INCLUDED (the value) and end with scheduling link. When sending pricing (after call attempt or international), use bullet lists and tables.

 16 + **Key rule:** Default to pushing for a Zoom call first. Send pricing in email only after trying for a call, if they push back, or for international leads. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 18 ## 2. Budget Constraints / Can't Afford

 19 **Trigger:** "Too expensive", "above our ability", "no budget", "can't spend"

 170 ## Drafting Rules

 171 - - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.

 171 + - **Default to pushing for a Zoom call before sending pricing.** Steven's first preference is always to get them on a call to discuss options and quote. If they push back or after already trying, send pricing in email. For internation

 + al leads, send pricing directly without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).

 172 - **NEVER fabricate or assume product features.** Only state things that are verified facts about CodeCombat. If unsure, use a frame ("We have a lot of options that might work for your students") rather than a specific claim. Getting

 caught making something up damages Steven's credibility.

 173 - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 on.

 174 - **Budget constraints:** Don't just dump free content immediately. First try to work with their budget, offer a discount, or suggest a pilot. Free content is the fallback, not the lead.

 ⏺ Fixed. The nuance is:

 - Domestic, first touch: Push for Zoom call, don't lead with pricing

 - Domestic, after trying / they push back: Send pricing in email

 - International: Send pricing directly (unless significant opp)

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 12 lines

 189 - Formal connectors: "However," "Nevertheless," "Consequently"

 190 - Hedging phrases: "It's worth noting that," "It's important to mention"

 192 + ## Learned Corrections (from draft review 2026-04-07)

 193 + - **Ask specific budget questions, not generic.** "What does your budget look like?" is too vague. Better: "Are you needing it to be under some specific amount for this purchase or are you trying to get it down to a specific price per

 + student?"

 194 + - **Add encouragement for budget-constrained prospects.** "Don't give up yet!" — Steven is optimistic and solution-oriented, not just accommodating.

 195 + - **Mention the grant writing specialist** when budget is the issue. Not just free content — active help finding money.

 196 + - **Frame CTAs with incentives.** Not just "let's chat" — "we'll go over all the options and I can even set you up with trial licenses."

 197 + - **Use "Let's connect via a zoom call whenever is convenient"** — warmer and less transactional than "Want to hop on a quick call?"

 198 + - **Keep free license offers flexible.** Say "for a little while" not a specific timeframe in the initial reply. Lock down details later.

 199 + - **Ask scoping questions on separate lines.** How long? How many students? What grades? Each gets its own line for readability.

 200 + - **Acknowledge time gaps.** "A lot has likely changed since we last connected" — validates the prospect's return and creates curiosity.

 201 + - **Always add a follow-up question that opens the door.** Even simple ones like "How many students do you have?" keep the conversation going.

 202 + - **Never fabricate product features.** If unsure about a capability, don't mention it. Use frames instead.

 203 +

 204 ---

 206 **Summary**: Steven writes like a helpful human, not a sales robot. He's efficient, empathetic, and adapts fluidly to each recipient's communication style. His emails prioritize clarity and actionability over polish, and he's comforta

 ble being brief when brevity serves the conversation.

 207 No newline at end of file

 ⏺ Really valuable feedback. Let me capture these learnings and redraft.

 Key corrections:

 - Never send pricing in email — always drive toward a call to discuss options and quote

 - Budget constraints — try to work with their budget / offer discount / pilot first, THEN mention free stuff

 - Don't fabricate product claims — only state verified facts. Frames are OK, fabricated features are not

 - Unlock content — Steven can unlock more free content + give ~30 days free licenses strategically

 - Tone — never risk sounding combative or dismissive, especially when someone expressed frustration

 6 ## 1. Pricing / Licensing Questions

 7 - **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, per-license, bulk pricing, quote, PO amount

 8 - **Steven's pattern:** Direct price answer first line → pricing breakdown → mention free content → soft CTA

 7 + **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, per-license, bulk pricing, quote, how licensing works

 8 + **Steven's pattern:** Almost NEVER sends pricing in email. Instead → acknowledge their interest → give a high-level sense of what's included → drive toward a call to discuss options, answer questions, and get them a fresh quote.

 9 **Real snippets:**

 10 - - "Our minimum purchase order is 10 annual licenses for $70 each ($700)."

 11 - - "For 120 licenses, the cost would be $50 x 120 licenses for 1 year = $6,000."

 12 - - "For 20 licenses, the cost would be as follows. $50 x 20 licenses for 1 year = $1000."

 10 + - "Want to hop on a quick call so I can walk you through everything and get you a fresh quote?"

 11 + - "I can go over all the options and pricing on a call. Pick any time here: [calendar link]"

 12 - "You have 3 options: the first two come with the teacher dashboard, progress tracking, and a plethora of teacher and student resources."

 14 - - "We also have a second option for you, which is a Self-Serve Purchase."

 15 - **Typical length:** Medium-long. Uses bullet lists and tables for pricing tiers. Always ends with scheduling link or soft CTA.

 16 - **Key detail:** Always mention the minimum is 10 licenses ($700) and that self-serve is an option for smaller orders.

 13 + **Typical length:** Medium. Focus on what's INCLUDED (the value), not specific dollar amounts. Always end with scheduling link.

 14 + **Key rule:** Do NOT include specific pricing numbers in email replies. Push for a call where Steven can discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 68 ## 6. Free Trial / Pilot Offers

 69 - **Trigger:** Prospect wants to try before buying, exploring options

 69 + **Trigger:** Prospect wants to try before buying, exploring options , asking for access past free levels

 70 + **Steven's pattern:** Offer free licenses directly → specify duration → mention free content. Steven can unlock additional free content and give ~30 days of free licenses to anyone at his discretion. He uses this strategically — when i

 + t will benefit the relationship or move the deal forward.

 167 >

 168 > *Through the end of September*, we will be offering a promo..."

 170 + ## Drafting Rules

 171 + - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.

 172 + - **NEVER fabricate or assume product features.** Only state things that are verified facts about CodeCombat. If unsure, use a frame ("We have a lot of options that might work for your students") rather than a specific claim. Getting

 + caught making something up damages Steven's credibility.

 173 + - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 + on.

 174 + - **Budget constraints:** Don't just dump free content immediately. First try to work with their budget, offer a discount, or suggest a pilot. Free content is the fallback, not the lead.

 175 + - **Drive toward calls:** Steven's strength is on the phone. Email is for opening doors and quick confirmations. Push toward scheduling a call whenever there's a real opportunity.

 176 +

 177 ## Anti-AI-Tell Checklist

 178 These are the most common patterns that make AI-drafted emails detectable. Violating any of these breaks the illusion:

 179 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 170 ## Drafting Rules

 171 - - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.

 + al leads, send pricing directly without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).

 173 - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 ⏺ Fixed. The nuance is:

 - Domestic, first touch: Push for Zoom call, don't lead with pricing

 - Domestic, after trying / they push back: Send pricing in email

 - International: Send pricing directly (unless significant opp)

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 12 lines

 189 - Formal connectors: "However," "Nevertheless," "Consequently"

 190 - Hedging phrases: "It's worth noting that," "It's important to mention"

 192 + ## Learned Corrections (from draft review 2026-04-07)

 193 + - **Ask specific budget questions, not generic.** "What does your budget look like?" is too vague. Better: "Are you needing it to be under some specific amount for this purchase or are you trying to get it down to a specific price per

 + student?"

 194 + - **Add encouragement for budget-constrained prospects.** "Don't give up yet!" — Steven is optimistic and solution-oriented, not just accommodating.

 195 + - **Mention the grant writing specialist** when budget is the issue. Not just free content — active help finding money.

 196 + - **Frame CTAs with incentives.** Not just "let's chat" — "we'll go over all the options and I can even set you up with trial licenses."

 197 + - **Use "Let's connect via a zoom call whenever is convenient"** — warmer and less transactional than "Want to hop on a quick call?"

 198 + - **Keep free license offers flexible.** Say "for a little while" not a specific timeframe in the initial reply. Lock down details later.

 199 + - **Ask scoping questions on separate lines.** How long? How many students? What grades? Each gets its own line for readability.

 200 + - **Acknowledge time gaps.** "A lot has likely changed since we last connected" — validates the prospect's return and creates curiosity.

 201 + - **Always add a follow-up question that opens the door.** Even simple ones like "How many students do you have?" keep the conversation going.

 202 + - **Never fabricate product features.** If unsure about a capability, don't mention it. Use frames instead.

 203 +

 204 ---

 206 **Summary**: Steven writes like a helpful human, not a sales robot. He's efficient, empathetic, and adapts fluidly to each recipient's communication style. His emails prioritize clarity and actionability over polish, and he's comforta

 ble being brief when brevity serves the conversation.

 207 No newline at end of file

 16 ## 2. Budget Constraints / Can't Afford

 17 **Trigger:** "Too expensive", "above our ability", "no budget", "can't spend"

 18 - **Steven's pattern:** Validate ("No problem at all") → offer free alternatives → list free content → mention grants → leave door open

 18 + **Steven's pattern:** Validate ("No problem at all") → first try to work with their budget or offer a discount/pilot → THEN mention free content and grants → leave door open. Don't just dump free stuff immediately — explore what might

 + work for them first.

 19 **Real snippets:**

 20 - "No problem at all. I'd be more than happy to give you some free licenses for 30 days if you'd like to dig in and explore it yourself or with your classes?"

 21 - "We also have tons of free stuff already built-in like: [bulleted list of free content]"

 66 **Calendar link:** https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 68 ## 6. Free Trial / Pilot Offers

 69 - **Trigger:** Prospect wants to try before buying, exploring options

 70 - **Steven's pattern:** Offer free licenses directly → specify duration → mention free content

 69 + **Trigger:** Prospect wants to try before buying, exploring options , asking for access past free levels

 70 + **Steven's pattern:** Offer free licenses directly → specify duration → mention free content. Steven can unlock additional free content and give ~30 days of free licenses to anyone at his discretion. He uses this strategically — when i

 + t will benefit the relationship or move the deal forward.

 71 **Real snippets:**

 72 - "I went ahead and granted you 125 licenses that will be active through the end of April."

 73 - "Sweet, I unlocked it, but also gave you 30 full licenses through April 18th."

 74 - "I'd be more than happy to give you some free licenses for 30 days if you'd like to dig in and explore it yourself or with your classes?"

 75 - "Since this is your first year using us, we will meet you wherever you are and offer you a pilot."

 76 **Typical length:** Short-medium. Direct action (already granted licenses) or concrete offer. No lengthy explanation of why pilots are great.

 77 + **Key detail:** Some content can be unlocked with a quick sales call, but Steven often just unlocks it if they ask via email. It just opens up a bit more free content.

 79 ## 7. No-Pressure Follow-up / Patience

 80 **Trigger:** Prospect is busy, delayed, or needs time

 167 >

 168 > *Through the end of September*, we will be offering a promo..."

 170 + ## Drafting Rules

 171 + - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.

 172 + - **NEVER fabricate or assume product features.** Only state things that are verified facts about CodeCombat. If unsure, use a frame ("We have a lot of options that might work for your students") rather than a specific claim. Getting

 + caught making something up damages Steven's credibility.

 173 + - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 + on.

 174 + - **Budget constraints:** Don't just dump free content immediately. First try to work with their budget, offer a discount, or suggest a pilot. Free content is the fallback, not the lead.

 175 + - **Drive toward calls:** Steven's strength is on the phone. Email is for opening doors and quick confirmations. Push toward scheduling a call whenever there's a real opportunity.

 176 +

 177 ## Anti-AI-Tell Checklist

 178 These are the most common patterns that make AI-drafted emails detectable. Violating any of these breaks the illusion:

 179 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 ⎿   Added 5 lines, removed 3 lines

 6 ## 1. Pricing / Licensing Questions

 7 **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, per-license, bulk pricing, quote, how licensing works

 8 - **Steven's pattern:** Almost NEVER sends pricing in email. Instead → acknowledge their interest → give a high-level sense of what's included → drive toward a call to discuss options, answer questions, and get them a fresh quote.

 8 + **Steven's pattern:** First preference is always to push for a Zoom call to discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. If they push back on a call or after already trying, Steven will send pricing in email. For internationa

 + l leads/contacts, send pricing directly in the email without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).

 9 **Real snippets:**

 10 - "Want to hop on a quick call so I can walk you through everything and get you a fresh quote?"

 11 - "I can go over all the options and pricing on a call. Pick any time here: [calendar link]"

 12 - "You have 3 options: the first two come with the teacher dashboard, progress tracking, and a plethora of teacher and student resources."

 13 - **Typical length:** Medium. Focus on what's INCLUDED (the value), not specific dollar amounts. Always end with scheduling link.

 14 - **Key rule:** Do NOT include specific pricing numbers in email replies. Push for a call where Steven can discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 13 + - "Our minimum purchase order is 10 annual licenses for $70 each ($700)."

 14 + - "For 20 licenses, the cost would be as follows. $50 x 20 licenses for 1 year = $1000."

 15 + **Typical length:** Medium. When pushing for a call, focus on what's INCLUDED (the value) and end with scheduling link. When sending pricing (after call attempt or international), use bullet lists and tables.

 16 + **Key rule:** Default to pushing for a Zoom call first. Send pricing in email only after trying for a call, if they push back, or for international leads. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 18 ## 2. Budget Constraints / Can't Afford

 19 **Trigger:** "Too expensive", "above our ability", "no budget", "can't spend"

 168 > *Through the end of September*, we will be offering a promo..."

 170 ## Drafting Rules

 171 - - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.

 171 + - **Default to pushing for a Zoom call before sending pricing.** Steven's first preference is always to get them on a call to discuss options and quote. If they push back or after already trying, send pricing in email. For internation

 + al leads, send pricing directly without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).

 172 - **NEVER fabricate or assume product features.** Only state things that are verified facts about CodeCombat. If unsure, use a frame ("We have a lot of options that might work for your students") rather than a specific claim. Getting

 caught making something up damages Steven's credibility.

 173 - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 on.

 174 - **Budget constraints:** Don't just dump free content immediately. First try to work with their budget, offer a discount, or suggest a pilot. Free content is the fallback, not the lead.

 ⏺ Fixed. The nuance is:

 - Domestic, first touch: Push for Zoom call, don't lead with pricing

 - Domestic, after trying / they push back: Send pricing in email

 - International: Send pricing directly (unless significant opp)

 The test drafts I already redrafted still hold — Kirk, Juniper, and Alice are all domestic first-touch situations where pushing for a call is correct. Want to approve these and create the Gmail drafts, or any other tweaks?

 How long will it run?

 What grades?

 ⏺ These are great. Let me capture the specific differences as learnings so future drafts are better.

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 12 lines

 189 - Formal connectors: "However," "Nevertheless," "Consequently"

 190 - Hedging phrases: "It's worth noting that," "It's important to mention"

 192 + ## Learned Corrections (from draft review 2026-04-07)

 193 + - **Ask specific budget questions, not generic.** "What does your budget look like?" is too vague. Better: "Are you needing it to be under some specific amount for this purchase or are you trying to get it down to a specific price per

 + student?"

 194 + - **Add encouragement for budget-constrained prospects.** "Don't give up yet!" — Steven is optimistic and solution-oriented, not just accommodating.

 195 + - **Mention the grant writing specialist** when budget is the issue. Not just free content — active help finding money.

 196 + - **Frame CTAs with incentives.** Not just "let's chat" — "we'll go over all the options and I can even set you up with trial licenses."

 197 + - **Use "Let's connect via a zoom call whenever is convenient"** — warmer and less transactional than "Want to hop on a quick call?"

 198 + - **Keep free license offers flexible.** Say "for a little while" not a specific timeframe in the initial reply. Lock down details later.

 199 + - **Ask scoping questions on separate lines.** How long? How many students? What grades? Each gets its own line for readability.

 200 + - **Acknowledge time gaps.** "A lot has likely changed since we last connected" — validates the prospect's return and creates curiosity.

 201 + - **Always add a follow-up question that opens the door.** Even simple ones like "How many students do you have?" keep the conversation going.

 202 + - **Never fabricate product features.** If unsure about a capability, don't mention it. Use frames instead.

 203 +

 204 ---

 206 **Summary**: Steven writes like a helpful human, not a sales robot. He's efficient, empathetic, and adapts fluidly to each recipient's communication style. His emails prioritize clarity and actionability over polish, and he's comforta

 ble being brief when brevity serves the conversation.

 207 No newline at end of file

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 7 lines

 167 >

 168 > *Through the end of September*, we will be offering a promo..."

 170 + ## Drafting Rules

 171 + - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.

 172 + - **NEVER fabricate or assume product features.** Only state things that are verified facts about CodeCombat. If unsure, use a frame ("We have a lot of options that might work for your students") rather than a specific claim. Getting

 + caught making something up damages Steven's credibility.

 173 + - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 + on.

 174 + - **Budget constraints:** Don't just dump free content immediately. First try to work with their budget, offer a discount, or suggest a pilot. Free content is the fallback, not the lead.

 175 + - **Drive toward calls:** Steven's strength is on the phone. Email is for opening doors and quick confirmations. Push toward scheduling a call whenever there's a real opportunity.

 176 +

 177 ## Anti-AI-Tell Checklist

 178 These are the most common patterns that make AI-drafted emails detectable. Violating any of these breaks the illusion:

 179 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 ⏺ Now the redrafts:

 ---

 1. Kirk Ellern (Budget Constraints) — REDRAFT:

 ▎ Hey Kirk,

 ▎ No problem at all. What does your budget look like? We work with schools in all kinds of situations and can usually find something that fits.

 ▎ We also offer pilots and discounted options depending on the size and timing. And if budget is completely locked right now, we have a bunch of free content your students can start using today.

 ▎ Want to hop on a quick call and I can go over all the options? Pick a time here: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 ---

 3. Juniper Gill (Pricing / Product Info) — REDRAFT:

 ▎ Hey Juniper,

 ▎ Got it, glad you're still interested! No worries about the school change.

 ▎ For 75-100 students, we have a few different options depending on what you're looking for. Our K-12 suite covers everything from block-based coding for younger students all the way through AP-level CS, plus we just launched an Algebra course and

 have Cybersecurity coming this fall.

 ▎ Want to hop on a quick call so I can walk you through everything and get you a fresh quote? Pick any time here: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 ---

 4. Alice Edwards (Free Trial / Pilot) — REDRAFT:

 ▎ Hey Alice,

 ▎ Got it, sorry the timing didn't work out last time. I can definitely set you up with free licenses for your summer ESY program starting June 11. Just let me know how many students you're expecting and I'll get those ready ahead of time.

 ▎ Want to do a quick call before then so I can walk you through the best setup for your program? Pick any time here: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 ---

 5. Stephanie Boring (Unlock Content) — REDRAFT:

 ▎ Hey Stephanie,

 ▎ I went ahead and unlocked some more content for you. You should be good to go past level 9 now!

 ▎ [STEVEN: Confirm you unlocked access for her before sending. If you also gave her 32 free licenses, mention that here.]

 ▎ Let me know if you need anything else or run into any issues.

 ---

 it but i just prefer to always push to pursuade them to get on a zoom call with me as my first preference. Also for almost all international leads and contacts i will just send the international pricing dierctly in the email and not even try to do a

 zoom call unless its a significant opportunity.

 ⏺ Got it — I overcorrected. The rule is a preference, not a hard ban. Let me fix this.

 ⏺ Update ( memory/response_playbook.md )

 ⎿   Added 5 lines, removed 3 lines

 6 ## 1. Pricing / Licensing Questions

 7 **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, per-license, bulk pricing, quote, how licensing works

 8 - **Steven's pattern:** Almost NEVER sends pricing in email. Instead → acknowledge their interest → give a high-level sense of what's included → drive toward a call to discuss options, answer questions, and get them a fresh quote.

 8 + **Steven's pattern:** First preference is always to push for a Zoom call to discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. If they push back on a call or after already trying, Steven will send pricing in email. For internationa

 + l leads/contacts, send pricing directly in the email without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).

 9 **Real snippets:**

 10 - "Want to hop on a quick call so I can walk you through everything and get you a fresh quote?"

 11 - "I can go over all the options and pricing on a call. Pick any time here: [calendar link]"

 12 - "You have 3 options: the first two come with the teacher dashboard, progress tracking, and a plethora of teacher and student resources."

 13 - **Typical length:** Medium. Focus on what's INCLUDED (the value), not specific dollar amounts. Always end with scheduling link.

 14 - **Key rule:** Do NOT include specific pricing numbers in email replies. Push for a call where Steven can discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 13 + - "Our minimum purchase order is 10 annual licenses for $70 each ($700)."

 14 + - "For 20 licenses, the cost would be as follows. $50 x 20 licenses for 1 year = $1000."

 15 + **Typical length:** Medium. When pushing for a call, focus on what's INCLUDED (the value) and end with scheduling link. When sending pricing (after call attempt or international), use bullet lists and tables.

 16 + **Key rule:** Default to pushing for a Zoom call first. Send pricing in email only after trying for a call, if they push back, or for international leads. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 18 ## 2. Budget Constraints / Can't Afford

 19 **Trigger:** "Too expensive", "above our ability", "no budget", "can't spend"

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 1 line, removed 1 line

 168 > *Through the end of September*, we will be offering a promo..."

 170 ## Drafting Rules

 171 - - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.

 171 + - **Default to pushing for a Zoom call before sending pricing.** Steven's first preference is always to get them on a call to discuss options and quote. If they push back or after already trying, send pricing in email. For internation

 + al leads, send pricing directly without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).

 172 - **NEVER fabricate or assume product features.** Only state things that are verified facts about CodeCombat. If unsure, use a frame ("We have a lot of options that might work for your students") rather than a specific claim. Getting

 caught making something up damages Steven's credibility.

 173 - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 on.

 174 - **Budget constraints:** Don't just dump free content immediately. First try to work with their budget, offer a discount, or suggest a pilot. Free content is the fallback, not the lead.

 ⏺ Fixed. The nuance is:

 - Domestic, first touch: Push for Zoom call, don't lead with pricing

 - Domestic, after trying / they push back: Send pricing in email

 - International: Send pricing directly (unless significant opp)

 The test drafts I already redrafted still hold — Kirk, Juniper, and Alice are all domestic first-touch situations where pushing for a call is correct. Want to approve these and create the Gmail drafts, or any other tweaks?
