 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 12 lines

 189 - Formal connectors: "However," "Nevertheless," "Consequently"

 190 - Hedging phrases: "It's worth noting that," "It's important to mention"

 192 + ## Learned Corrections (from draft review 2026-04-07)

 193 + - **Ask specific budget questions, not generic.** "What does your budget look like?" is too vague. Better: "Are you needing it to be under some specific amount for this purchase or are you trying to get it down to a specific price per

 + student?"

 194 + - **Add encouragement for budget-constrained prospects.** "Don't give up yet!" — Steven is optimistic and solution-oriented, not just accommodating.

 195 + - **Mention the grant writing specialist** when budget is the issue. Not just free content — active help finding money.

 196 + - **Frame CTAs with incentives.** Not just "let's chat" — "we'll go over all the options and I can even set you up with trial licenses."

 197 + - **Use "Let's connect via a zoom call whenever is convenient"** — warmer and less transactional than "Want to hop on a quick call?"

 198 + - **Keep free license offers flexible.** Say "for a little while" not a specific timeframe in the initial reply. Lock down details later.

 199 + - **Ask scoping questions on separate lines.** How long? How many students? What grades? Each gets its own line for readability.

 200 + - **Acknowledge time gaps.** "A lot has likely changed since we last connected" — validates the prospect's return and creates curiosity.

 201 + - **Always add a follow-up question that opens the door.** Even simple ones like "How many students do you have?" keep the conversation going.

 202 + - **Never fabricate product features.** If unsure about a capability, don't mention it. Use frames instead.

 203 +

 204 ---

 206 **Summary**: Steven writes like a helpful human, not a sales robot. He's efficient, empathetic, and adapts fluidly to each recipient's communication style. His emails prioritize clarity and actionability over polish, and he's comforta

 ble being brief when brevity serves the conversation.

 207 No newline at end of file

 59 - **Draft:**

 61 - ```

 62 - Steven approves, edits, or skips each one.

 53 + ## Step 4: Create Gmail drafts IMMEDIATELY

 54 + Do NOT show drafts in Claude Code for approval first. Create them directly in Gmail so Steven can open each email and see the draft reply waiting. Steven's workflow: open inbox → open email → see draft → tweak → send → next.

 57 ```

 59 ```

 179 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 ⏺ Update ( memory/response_playbook.md )

 ⎿   Added 5 lines, removed 3 lines

 6 ## 1. Pricing / Licensing Questions

 7 **Trigger:** Asks about cost, per-seat, per-license, bulk pricing, quote, how licensing works

 8 - **Steven's pattern:** Almost NEVER sends pricing in email. Instead → acknowledge their interest → give a high-level sense of what's included → drive toward a call to discuss options, answer questions, and get them a fresh quote.

 8 + **Steven's pattern:** First preference is always to push for a Zoom call to discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. If they push back on a call or after already trying, Steven will send pricing in email. For internationa

 + l leads/contacts, send pricing directly in the email without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).

 9 **Real snippets:**

 10 - "Want to hop on a quick call so I can walk you through everything and get you a fresh quote?"

 11 - "I can go over all the options and pricing on a call. Pick any time here: [calendar link]"

 12 - "You have 3 options: the first two come with the teacher dashboard, progress tracking, and a plethora of teacher and student resources."

 13 - **Typical length:** Medium. Focus on what's INCLUDED (the value), not specific dollar amounts. Always end with scheduling link.

 14 - **Key rule:** Do NOT include specific pricing numbers in email replies. Push for a call where Steven can discuss options, answer questions, and tailor a quote. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 13 + - "Our minimum purchase order is 10 annual licenses for $70 each ($700)."

 14 + - "For 20 licenses, the cost would be as follows. $50 x 20 licenses for 1 year = $1000."

 15 + **Typical length:** Medium. When pushing for a call, focus on what's INCLUDED (the value) and end with scheduling link. When sending pricing (after call attempt or international), use bullet lists and tables.

 16 + **Key rule:** Default to pushing for a Zoom call first. Send pricing in email only after trying for a call, if they push back, or for international leads. Calendar link: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 18 ## 2. Budget Constraints / Can't Afford

 19 **Trigger:** "Too expensive", "above our ability", "no budget", "can't spend"

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 1 line, removed 1 line

 168 > *Through the end of September*, we will be offering a promo..."

 170 ## Drafting Rules

 171 - - **NEVER include specific pricing numbers in email replies.** Always push for a call to discuss options and get a fresh quote. Use calendar link.

 171 + - **Default to pushing for a Zoom call before sending pricing.** Steven's first preference is always to get them on a call to discuss options and quote. If they push back or after already trying, send pricing in email. For internation

 + al leads, send pricing directly without pushing for a call (unless it's a significant opportunity).

 172 - **NEVER fabricate or assume product features.** Only state things that are verified facts about CodeCombat. If unsure, use a frame ("We have a lot of options that might work for your students") rather than a specific claim. Getting

 caught making something up damages Steven's credibility.

 173 - **NEVER risk sounding combative or dismissive.** Especially when someone expressed frustration or a negative experience. Don't reframe their problem as not a problem ("a month is plenty of time"). Validate first, then offer a soluti

 on.

 174 - **Budget constraints:** Don't just dump free content immediately. First try to work with their budget, offer a discount, or suggest a pilot. Free content is the fallback, not the lead.

 ⏺ Fixed. The nuance is:

 - Domestic, first touch: Push for Zoom call, don't lead with pricing

 - Domestic, after trying / they push back: Send pricing in email

 - International: Send pricing directly (unless significant opp)

 The test drafts I already redrafted still hold — Kirk, Juniper, and Alice are all domestic first-touch situations where pushing for a call is correct. Want to approve these and create the Gmail drafts, or any other tweaks?

 ▎ Hey Kirk,

 ▎ No problem at all. What does your budget look like? Are you needing it to be under some specific amount for this purchase or are you trying to get it down to a specific price per student?

 We work with schools in all kinds of budget situations and can almost always find something that fits!

 ▎ We also offer pilots and discounted options depending on the size and timing.

 And if budget is completely locked right now, we have a TON of free content your students can start using today and a grant writing specialist to help you plan for next semester.

 Either way - don’t give up yet!

 ▎ Pick a time here - we’ll go over all the options and i can even set you up with trial licenses for a while: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 How long will it run?

 What grades?

 Did you have any subjects or products in mind already that you wanted to use?

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 12 lines

 189 - Formal connectors: "However," "Nevertheless," "Consequently"

 190 - Hedging phrases: "It's worth noting that," "It's important to mention"

 192 + ## Learned Corrections (from draft review 2026-04-07)

 193 + - **Ask specific budget questions, not generic.** "What does your budget look like?" is too vague. Better: "Are you needing it to be under some specific amount for this purchase or are you trying to get it down to a specific price per

 + student?"

 194 + - **Add encouragement for budget-constrained prospects.** "Don't give up yet!" — Steven is optimistic and solution-oriented, not just accommodating.

 195 + - **Mention the grant writing specialist** when budget is the issue. Not just free content — active help finding money.

 196 + - **Frame CTAs with incentives.** Not just "let's chat" — "we'll go over all the options and I can even set you up with trial licenses."

 197 + - **Use "Let's connect via a zoom call whenever is convenient"** — warmer and less transactional than "Want to hop on a quick call?"

 198 + - **Keep free license offers flexible.** Say "for a little while" not a specific timeframe in the initial reply. Lock down details later.

 199 + - **Ask scoping questions on separate lines.** How long? How many students? What grades? Each gets its own line for readability.

 200 + - **Acknowledge time gaps.** "A lot has likely changed since we last connected" — validates the prospect's return and creates curiosity.

 201 + - **Always add a follow-up question that opens the door.** Even simple ones like "How many students do you have?" keep the conversation going.

 202 + - **Never fabricate product features.** If unsure about a capability, don't mention it. Use frames instead.

 203 +

 204 ---

 206 **Summary**: Steven writes like a helpful human, not a sales robot. He's efficient, empathetic, and adapts fluidly to each recipient's communication style. His emails prioritize clarity and actionability over polish, and he's comforta

 ble being brief when brevity serves the conversation.

 207 No newline at end of file

 ⎿   Added 13 lines, removed 13 lines

 50 6. End naturally — Steven often just stops after the last point, no formal sign-off needed

 51 7. For FLAG emails, include `[STEVEN: ...]` inline where Steven needs to fill in info

 53 - ## Step 4: Present for review

 54 - Show Steven each email in this format:

 55 - ```

 56 - **From:** sender name <email>

 57 - **Subject:** Re: subject line

 58 - **Classification:** DRAFT | FLAG | SKIP

 59 - **Draft:**

 60 - [the draft reply text]

 61 - ```

 62 - Steven approves, edits, or skips each one.

 53 + ## Step 4: Create Gmail drafts IMMEDIATELY

 54 + Do NOT show drafts in Claude Code for approval first. Create them directly in Gmail so Steven can open each email and see the draft reply waiting. Steven's workflow: open inbox → open email → see draft → tweak → send → next.

 64 - ## Step 5: Create Gmail drafts

 65 - For approved emails:

 56 + For each DRAFT or FLAG email:

 57 ```

 67 - gmail_create_draft(to=sender_email, body= approved_text , threadId=thread_id, cc=original_cc_recipients)

 58 + gmail_create_draft(to=sender_email, body= draft_text , threadId=thread_id, cc=original_cc_recipients)

 59 ```

 61 **CC handling:** Include all original CC recipients from the inbound email, minus any noreply/automated addresses. If the inbound was sent to multiple people including Steven, reply-all.

 63 + After creating all drafts, show Steven a brief summary of what was drafted:

 64 + ```

 65 + **Drafted 5 replies, skipped 3:**

 66 + 1. Kate Grow — AI question → product info + call CTA

 67 + 2. Anthony Pollina — locked out of levels → FLAG [needs: check licensing vs bug]

 68 + 3. Krista Farris — "wasn't me" → pivoted to her as CS teacher

 69 + SKIPPED: Arlington ISD (bid notification), Megan Weber (OOO), PandaDoc reminder

 70 + ```

 71 +

 ⏺ Good call. Adding to the banned list now. Here are 10 alternatives that fit your voice:

 1. "Let's connect via Zoom whenever works for you" (your actual phrasing from earlier)

 2. "Want to do a quick Zoom so I can walk you through everything?"

 3. "Pick a time here and I'll show you what I mean: [link]"

 4. "I can show you all of this on a quick Zoom. Pick any time here: [link]"

 5. "Easiest way to go over this is on a Zoom. Here's my calendar: [link]"

 6. "Let me show you on a Zoom. Pick a time that works: [link]"

 7. "Want to connect via Zoom this week? Here's my calendar: [link]"

 8. "Happy to walk you through everything. Pick a time here: [link]"

 9. "I'll show you everything on a Zoom. Grab a time here: [link]"

 10. "Let's do a Zoom and I can answer all your questions. Pick a time: [link]"

 ⎿   Added 2 lines

 179 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 180 - Semicolons. Steven never uses them.

 181 - "I'd be happy to" / "I'd love to" / "Absolutely!" / "Great question!" / "That's a great point"

 182 + - "Want to hop on a quick call" — overused salesy vendor phrase. Use alternatives: "Let's connect via Zoom", "I can show you on a quick Zoom", "Pick a time here and I'll walk you through it", etc.

 183 + - It's always a Zoom call, not a phone call. Say "Zoom" not "call" when referring to meetings.

 184 - "I hope this finds you well" / "I wanted to reach out" / "Just circling back" / "per our conversation"

 185 - Corporate vocabulary: leverage, streamline, robust, comprehensive, cutting-edge, foster, delve, facilitate, utilize

 186 - Starting 2+ consecutive sentences with "I"

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 1 line, removed 1 line

 179 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 180 - Semicolons. Steven never uses them.

 181 - "I'd be happy to" / "I'd love to" / "Absolutely!" / "Great question!" / "That's a great point"

 182 - - "Want to hop on a quick call" — overused salesy vendor phrase. Use alternatives : "Let's connect via Zoom", "I can show you on a quick Zoom", "Pick a time here and I'll walk you through it" , etc .

 182 + - "Want to hop on a quick call" — overused salesy vendor phrase. Preferred alternative : " Want to connect via Zoom this week? Here's my calendar: [link]". Other good options: " Let's connect via Zoom", "I can show you on a quick Zoom",

 + " Let me show you on a Zoom", " Pick a time here and I'll walk you through it" . Never use #5 style "Easiest way to . .."

 183 - It's always a Zoom call, not a phone call. Say "Zoom" not "call" when referring to meetings.

 184 - "I hope this finds you well" / "I wanted to reach out" / "Just circling back" / "per our conversation"

 185 - Corporate vocabulary: leverage, streamline, robust, comprehensive, cutting-edge, foster, delve, facilitate, utilize

 ⏺ These are great. Let me capture the specific differences as learnings so future drafts are better.

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 12 lines

 189 - Formal connectors: "However," "Nevertheless," "Consequently"

 190 - Hedging phrases: "It's worth noting that," "It's important to mention"

 192 + ## Learned Corrections (from draft review 2026-04-07)

 193 + - **Ask specific budget questions, not generic.** "What does your budget look like?" is too vague. Better: "Are you needing it to be under some specific amount for this purchase or are you trying to get it down to a specific price per

 + student?"

 194 + - **Add encouragement for budget-constrained prospects.** "Don't give up yet!" — Steven is optimistic and solution-oriented, not just accommodating.

 195 + - **Mention the grant writing specialist** when budget is the issue. Not just free content — active help finding money.

 196 + - **Frame CTAs with incentives.** Not just "let's chat" — "we'll go over all the options and I can even set you up with trial licenses."

 197 + - **Use "Let's connect via a zoom call whenever is convenient"** — warmer and less transactional than "Want to hop on a quick call?"

 198 + - **Keep free license offers flexible.** Say "for a little while" not a specific timeframe in the initial reply. Lock down details later.

 199 + - **Ask scoping questions on separate lines.** How long? How many students? What grades? Each gets its own line for readability.

 200 + - **Acknowledge time gaps.** "A lot has likely changed since we last connected" — validates the prospect's return and creates curiosity.

 201 + - **Always add a follow-up question that opens the door.** Even simple ones like "How many students do you have?" keep the conversation going.

 202 + - **Never fabricate product features.** If unsure about a capability, don't mention it. Use frames instead.

 203 +

 204 ---

 206 **Summary**: Steven writes like a helpful human, not a sales robot. He's efficient, empathetic, and adapts fluidly to each recipient's communication style. His emails prioritize clarity and actionability over polish, and he's comforta

 ble being brief when brevity serves the conversation.

 207 No newline at end of file

 The system is live. Any Claude Code session where you say "draft my emails" will follow the workflow — search unread, read threads, classify, draft in your voice, present for review, create threaded Gmail drafts. "Learn from my sends" compares drafts

 to what you actually sent and compounds corrections.

 Come up with 10 other ways we can say this. also its a zoom call not a phone call.

 ⏺ Good call. Adding to the banned list now. Here are 10 alternatives that fit your voice:

 1. "Let's connect via Zoom whenever works for you" (your actual phrasing from earlier)

 2. "Want to do a quick Zoom so I can walk you through everything?"

 3. "Pick a time here and I'll show you what I mean: [link]"

 4. "I can show you all of this on a quick Zoom. Pick any time here: [link]"

 5. "Easiest way to go over this is on a Zoom. Here's my calendar: [link]"

 6. "Let me show you on a Zoom. Pick a time that works: [link]"

 7. "Want to connect via Zoom this week? Here's my calendar: [link]"

 8. "Happy to walk you through everything. Pick a time here: [link]"

 9. "I'll show you everything on a Zoom. Grab a time here: [link]"

 10. "Let's do a Zoom and I can answer all your questions. Pick a time: [link]"

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 2 lines

 179 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 180 - Semicolons. Steven never uses them.

 181 - "I'd be happy to" / "I'd love to" / "Absolutely!" / "Great question!" / "That's a great point"

 182 + - "Want to hop on a quick call" — overused salesy vendor phrase. Use alternatives: "Let's connect via Zoom", "I can show you on a quick Zoom", "Pick a time here and I'll walk you through it", etc.

 183 + - It's always a Zoom call, not a phone call. Say "Zoom" not "call" when referring to meetings.

 184 - "I hope this finds you well" / "I wanted to reach out" / "Just circling back" / "per our conversation"

 185 - Corporate vocabulary: leverage, streamline, robust, comprehensive, cutting-edge, foster, delve, facilitate, utilize

 186 - Starting 2+ consecutive sentences with "I"

 + " Let me show you on a Zoom", " Pick a time here and I'll walk you through it" . Never use #5 style "Easiest way to . .."

 1. From: Kate Grow raisethebarctr@gmail.com

 Subject: Re: Kate, coding at Go Grow Homeschool.com

 Classification: DRAFT (Category: Product Info)

 Context: Kate runs a homeschool program. She replied to Steven's cold outreach asking "What are you guys doing with AI?" — short and casual.

 Draft:

 ▎ Hey Kate,

 ▎ Doing well, thanks! We've actually been doing a lot with AI. We launched an AI HackStack where students learn prompt engineering, we have a brand new AI Algebra course that just came out in March, and Cybersecurity is coming this fall.

 ▎ On top of that, our AI League lets students code AI bots that compete in global tournaments with real prizes.

 ▎ Want to hop on a quick call and I can show you everything? Pick a time here: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 ---

 2. From: Anthony Pollina apollina@lfschools.net

 Subject: Re: Deer Path Middle School-East + CodeCombat

 Classification: FLAG (Category: Support / Technical Routing)

 Context: Anthony is an existing user whose 7th grade Python students are locked out of level 10. He says he assigned them through level 26 but the map only shows challenge levels. This is a technical/account issue Steven should route to support.

 Draft:

 ▎ Hey Anthony,

 ▎ Sorry about that! Let me look into this for you.

 ▎ [STEVEN: Check if this is a licensing issue (free content cap at level 9) or a bug. If licensing, you can unlock more content or give him 30-day trial licenses. If it's a bug, CC support@codecombat.com or Noah.]

 ▎ In the meantime, can you tell me how many students you have? I want to make sure we get this sorted out for you quickly.

 ---

 3. From: Krista Farris kfarris@shawnee.k12.ok.us

 Subject: Re: Shawnee Middle School's CodeCombat request

 Classification: DRAFT (Category: Information Gathering)

 Context: Steven's outreach said "someone at Shawnee Middle School requested a license." Krista replied "It wasn't me, I wonder who it was?" — short, curious. Her sig says she's Computer Technology teacher at Shawnee MS.

 Draft:

 ▎ Ha, good question! Not sure who it was but since you're the Computer Technology teacher it sounds like it would land with you anyway 😉

 ▎ Would you be interested in checking it out? I can set you up with some free content to explore or we can hop on a quick call and I can walk you through everything.

 ▎ Pick a time here if you're interested: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 ---

 ⏺ Here are the 3 drafts:

 ---

 1. From: Kate Grow raisethebarctr@gmail.com

 Subject: Re: Kate, coding at Go Grow Homeschool.com

 Classification: DRAFT (Category: Product Info)

 Context: Kate runs a homeschool program. She replied to Steven's cold outreach asking "What are you guys doing with AI?" — short and casual.

 Draft:

 ▎ Hey Kate,

 ▎ Doing well, thanks! We've actually been doing a lot with AI. We launched an AI HackStack where students learn prompt engineering, we have a brand new AI Algebra course that just came out in March, and Cybersecurity is coming this fall.

 ▎ On top of that, our AI League lets students code AI bots that compete in global tournaments with real prizes.

 ▎ Want to hop on a quick call and I can show you everything? Pick a time here: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 ---

 2. From: Anthony Pollina apollina@lfschools.net

 Subject: Re: Deer Path Middle School-East + CodeCombat

 Classification: FLAG (Category: Support / Technical Routing)

 Context: Anthony is an existing user whose 7th grade Python students are locked out of level 10. He says he assigned them through level 26 but the map only shows challenge levels. This is a technical/account issue Steven should route to support.

 Draft:

 ▎ Hey Anthony,

 ▎ Sorry about that! Let me look into this for you.

 ▎ [STEVEN: Check if this is a licensing issue (free content cap at level 9) or a bug. If licensing, you can unlock more content or give him 30-day trial licenses. If it's a bug, CC support@codecombat.com or Noah.]

 ▎ In the meantime, can you tell me how many students you have? I want to make sure we get this sorted out for you quickly.

 ---

 3. From: Krista Farris kfarris@shawnee.k12.ok.us

 Subject: Re: Shawnee Middle School's CodeCombat request

 Classification: DRAFT (Category: Information Gathering)

 Context: Steven's outreach said "someone at Shawnee Middle School requested a license." Krista replied "It wasn't me, I wonder who it was?" — short, curious. Her sig says she's Computer Technology teacher at Shawnee MS.

 Draft:

 ▎ Hey Krista,

 ▎ Ha, good question! Not sure who it was but since you're the Computer Technology teacher it sounds like it would land with you anyway 😉

 ▎ Would you be interested in checking it out? I can set you up with some free content to explore or we can hop on a quick call and I can walk you through everything.

 ▎ Pick a time here if you're interested: https://codecombat.orumbriel.com/c/steven

 ---

 How do these land?

 ⏺ Update ( prompts/reply_draft.md )

 ⎿   Added 13 lines, removed 13 lines

 50 6. End naturally — Steven often just stops after the last point, no formal sign-off needed

 51 7. For FLAG emails, include `[STEVEN: ...]` inline where Steven needs to fill in info

 53 - ## Step 4: Present for review

 54 - Show Steven each email in this format:

 55 - ```

 56 - **From:** sender name <email>

 57 - **Subject:** Re: subject line

 58 - **Classification:** DRAFT | FLAG | SKIP

 59 - **Draft:**

 60 - [the draft reply text]

 61 - ```

 62 - Steven approves, edits, or skips each one.

 53 + ## Step 4: Create Gmail drafts IMMEDIATELY

 54 + Do NOT show drafts in Claude Code for approval first. Create them directly in Gmail so Steven can open each email and see the draft reply waiting. Steven's workflow: open inbox → open email → see draft → tweak → send → next.

 64 - ## Step 5: Create Gmail drafts

 65 - For approved emails:

 56 + For each DRAFT or FLAG email:

 57 ```

 67 - gmail_create_draft(to=sender_email, body= approved_text , threadId=thread_id, cc=original_cc_recipients)

 58 + gmail_create_draft(to=sender_email, body= draft_text , threadId=thread_id, cc=original_cc_recipients)

 59 ```

 61 **CC handling:** Include all original CC recipients from the inbound email, minus any noreply/automated addresses. If the inbound was sent to multiple people including Steven, reply-all.

 63 + After creating all drafts, show Steven a brief summary of what was drafted:

 64 + ```

 58 gmail_create_draft(to=sender_email, body=draft_html, thread Id=thread_id, cc=original_cc_recipients, content Type="text/html")

 ❯ make a note - i hate this phrase "Want to hop on a quick call" its used to commonly and is exactly the type of common salsesly phrasing from every vendor that educators get.

 Come up with 10 other ways we can say this. also its a zoom call not a phone call.

 ⏺ Good call. Adding to the banned list now. Here are 10 alternatives that fit your voice:

 1. "Let's connect via Zoom whenever works for you" (your actual phrasing from earlier)

 2. "Want to do a quick Zoom so I can walk you through everything?"

 3. "Pick a time here and I'll show you what I mean: [link]"

 4. "I can show you all of this on a quick Zoom. Pick any time here: [link]"

 5. "Easiest way to go over this is on a Zoom. Here's my calendar: [link]"

 6. "Let me show you on a Zoom. Pick a time that works: [link]"

 7. "Want to connect via Zoom this week? Here's my calendar: [link]"

 8. "Happy to walk you through everything. Pick a time here: [link]"

 9. "I'll show you everything on a Zoom. Grab a time here: [link]"

 10. "Let's do a Zoom and I can answer all your questions. Pick a time: [link]"

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 2 lines

 179 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 180 - Semicolons. Steven never uses them.

 181 - "I'd be happy to" / "I'd love to" / "Absolutely!" / "Great question!" / "That's a great point"

 182 + - "Want to hop on a quick call" — overused salesy vendor phrase. Use alternatives: "Let's connect via Zoom", "I can show you on a quick Zoom", "Pick a time here and I'll walk you through it", etc.

 183 + - It's always a Zoom call, not a phone call. Say "Zoom" not "call" when referring to meetings.

 184 - "I hope this finds you well" / "I wanted to reach out" / "Just circling back" / "per our conversation"

 185 - Corporate vocabulary: leverage, streamline, robust, comprehensive, cutting-edge, foster, delve, facilitate, utilize

 186 - Starting 2+ consecutive sentences with "I"

 ⏺ Noted and saved. "Want to hop on a quick call" is banned, and all meeting references should say Zoom, not call. How are those 3 drafts looking in Gmail?

 ⏺ Update ( memory/voice_profile.md )

 ⎿   Added 1 line, removed 1 line

 179 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 180 - Semicolons. Steven never uses them.

 181 - "I'd be happy to" / "I'd love to" / "Absolutely!" / "Great question!" / "That's a great point"

 182 - - "Want to hop on a quick call" — overused salesy vendor phrase. Use alternatives : "Let's connect via Zoom", "I can show you on a quick Zoom", "Pick a time here and I'll walk you through it" , etc .

 182 + - "Want to hop on a quick call" — overused salesy vendor phrase. Preferred alternative : " Want to connect via Zoom this week? Here's my calendar: [link]". Other good options: " Let's connect via Zoom", "I can show you on a quick Zoom",

 + " Let me show you on a Zoom", " Pick a time here and I'll walk you through it" . Never use #5 style "Easiest way to . .."

 183 - It's always a Zoom call, not a phone call. Say "Zoom" not "call" when referring to meetings.

 184 - "I hope this finds you well" / "I wanted to reach out" / "Just circling back" / "per our conversation"

 185 - Corporate vocabulary: leverage, streamline, robust, comprehensive, cutting-edge, foster, delve, facilitate, utilize

 Kate first, then I'll send a new one.

 ⏺ For step 2, here's exactly what to add:

 In the switch block near line 55, add this case after search_inbox_full :

 /**

 * Deletes a draft by its ID.

 * params: { draft_id (str) }

 */

 function deleteDraft(params) {

 var draftId = params.draft_id;

 if (!draftId) {

 return { success : false , error : "draft_id is required" };

 }

 var drafts = Gmail App.get Drafts();

 for ( var i = 0 ; i < drafts.length; i++) {

 if (drafts[i].get Id() === draft Id) {

 drafts[i].deleteDraft();

 return { success : true , deleted : draftId };

 }

 }

 return { success : false , error : "Draft not found: " + draftId };

 }

 Save with Cmd+S.

 For step 3:

 1. Click Deploy (blue button, top right)

 2. Click Manage deployments

 3. Click the pencil icon (edit) on the existing deployment

 4. In the Version dropdown, select New version

 5. Click Deploy

 That's it. Once you see the "successfully updated" message, tell me and I'll delete those 3 orphaned drafts.

 ⏺ Here's the full file. Select all in your Code.gs editor (Cmd+A) and replace with this:

 ⏺ Bash (cat /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout/gas/Code.gs)

 ⎿   /**

 * This is your private API that lives inside Google Apps Script.

 * It runs as YOU, with full access to your Gmail, Calendar, and Slides.

 * Deploy as a Web App (execute as Me, access to Anyone) and protect

 * with the secret token below.

 * HOW TO DEPLOY:

 * 1. Go to script.google.com → New Project → name it "Scout Bridge"

 * 2. Paste this entire file into the editor (replace the default code)

 * 3. Change SECRET_TOKEN below to any long random string you make up

 * 4. Click Deploy → New Deployment → Web App

 * - Execute as: Me (steven@codecombat.com)

 * - Who has access: Anyone

 * 5. Click Deploy → copy the Web App URL

 * 6. Set GAS_WEBHOOK_URL = that URL in Railway

 * 7. Set GAS_SECRET_TOKEN = your chosen token in Railway

 */

 // ─── CHANGE THIS — make it a long random string ───────────────────────────────

 var SECRET_TOKEN = "REPLACE_WITH_YOUR_SECRET_TOKEN_HERE";

 // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

 /**

 * Entry point for all POST requests from Scout.

 * Expects JSON body: { token, action, params }

 */

 function doPost(e) {

 try {

 var payload = JSON.parse(e.postData.contents);

 // Auth check

 if (payload.token !== SECRET_TOKEN) {

 return jsonResponse({ success: false, error: "Unauthorized" }, 401);

 }

 var action = payload.action;

 var params = payload.params || {};

 // Route to the right handler

 switch (action) {

 // ── Gmail ──────────────────────────────────────────────────

 case "get_sent_emails":

 return json Response(get Sent Emails(params));

 case "create_draft":

 return json Response(create Draft(params));

 case "search_inbox":

 return json Response(search Inbox(params));

 case "search_inbox_full":

 return json Response(search Inbox Full(params));

 case "delete_draft":

 return json Response(delete Draft(params));

 // ── Calendar ───────────────────────────────────────────────

 case "get_calendar_events":

 return json Response(get Calendar Events(params));

 case "create_calendar_event":

 return json Response(create Calendar Event(params));

 case "log_call":

 return json Response(log Call(params));

 // ── Slides ─────────────────────────────────────────────────

 case "create_presentation":

 return json Response(create Presentation(params));

 case "create_district_deck":

 return json Response(create District Deck(params));

 // ── Health check ───────────────────────────────────────────

 case "ping":

 return jsonResponse({ success: true, message: "Scout Bridge is live", user: "steven@codecombat.com" });

 // ── Phase 5: Google Docs ────────────────────────────────────

 case "create Google Doc":

 return json Response(create Google Doc(params));

 default:

 return jsonResponse({ success: false, error: "Unknown action: " + action });

 }

 } catch (err) {

 return json Response({ success: false, error: err.to String() });

 }

 }

 // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

 // GMAIL FUNCTIONS

 // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

 /**

 * Fetches sent emails from the last N months for voice training.

 * params: { months_back (int), max_results (int) }

 */

 function get Sent Emails(params) {

 var monthsBack = params.months_back || 6;

 var maxResults = params.max_results || 200;

 var cutoff = new Date();

 cutoff.set Month(cutoff.get Month() - months Back);

 var after Date = Utilities.format Date(cutoff, "UTC", "yyyy/MM/dd");

 var query = "in:sent after:" + afterDate;

 var threads = Gmail App.search(query, 0, max Results);

 var emails = [];

 for (var i = 0; i < threads.length; i++) {

 var messages = threads[i].getMessages();

 // Only grab the first (most recent) message per thread

 var msg = messages[messages.length - 1];

 var body = msg.get Plain Body();

 if (!body || body.length < 80) continue;

 // Skip automated/calendar emails

 var lower Body = body.to Lower Case();

 var lower Subject = msg.get Subject().to Lower Case();

 var combined = lower Body + lower Subject;

 var skipKeywords = ["unsubscribe", "calendar invite", "zoom.us/j/", "auto-reply",

 "out of office", "notification", "noreply", "no-reply", "donotreply"];

 var skip = false;

 for (var k = 0; k < skipKeywords.length; k++) {

 if (combined.index Of(skip Keywords[k]) !== -1) { skip = true; break; }

 }

 if (skip) continue;

 emails.push({

 subject: msg.getSubject(),

 to: msg.getTo(),

 date: msg.get Date().to String(),

 body: body.substring(0, 3000) // Cap per email to avoid massive payloads

 });

 }

 return { success: true, emails: emails, count: emails.length };

 }

 /**

 * Creates a Gmail draft.

 * params: { to (str), subject (str), body (str) }

 */

 function createDraft(params) {

 var to = params.to || "";

 var subject = params.subject || "(no subject)";

 var body = params.body || "";

 var draft = Gmail App.create Draft(to, subject, body);

 var draft Id = draft.get Id();

 return {

 success: true,

 draft_id: draftId,

 to: to,

 subject: subject,

 link: "https://mail.google.com/mail/u/0/#drafts"

 };

 }

 /**

 * Deletes a draft by its ID.

 * params: { draft_id (str) }

 */

 function deleteDraft(params) {

 var draftId = params.draft_id;

 if (!draftId) {

 return { success: false, error: "draft_id is required" };

 }

 var drafts = Gmail App.get Drafts();

 for (var i = 0; i < drafts.length; i++) {

 if (drafts[i].get Id() === draft Id) {

 drafts[i].deleteDraft();

 return { success: true, deleted: draftId };

 }

 }

 return { success: false, error: "Draft not found: " + draftId };

 }

 /**

 * Searches the inbox.

 * params: { query (str), max_results (int) }

 */

 function searchInbox(params) {

 var query = params.query || "";

 var maxResults = params.max_results || 10;

 var threads = Gmail App.search(query, 0, max Results);

 var results = [];

 for (var i = 0; i < threads.length; i++) {

 var msg = threads[i].getMessages()[0];

 results.push({

 subject: msg.getSubject(),

 from: msg.getFrom(),

 date: msg.get Date().to String(),

 snippet: msg.get Plain Body().substring(0, 300)

 });

 }

 return { success: true, results: results, count: results.length };

 }

 /**

 * Searches inbox and returns FULL email bodies with pagination.

 * params: { query (str), max_results (int), page_start (int), body_limit (int) }

 * body_limit defaults to 5000 chars per email. Set higher for full content.

 */

 function search Inbox Full(params) {

 var query = params.query || "";

 var maxResults = params.max_results || 20;

 var pageStart = params.page_start || 0;

 var bodyLimit = params.body_limit || 5000;

 var threads = Gmail App.search(query, page Start, max Results);

 var results = [];

 for (var i = 0; i < threads.length; i++) {

 var msg = threads[i].getMessages()[0];

 var body = msg.get Plain Body() || "";

 results.push({

 subject: msg.getSubject(),

 from: msg.getFrom(),

 date: msg.get Date().to String(),

 body: body.substring(0, bodyLimit),

 message_id: msg.getId(),

 thread_id: threads[i].getId(),

 labels: threads[i].get Labels().map(function(l) { return l.get Name(); })

 });

 }

 var has More = threads.length >= max Results;

 return {

 success: true,

 results: results,

 count: results.length,

 page_start: pageStart,

 has_more: hasMore

 };

 }

 // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

 // CALENDAR FUNCTIONS

 // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

 /**

 * Gets calendar events for a date range.

 * params: { days_ahead (int) } — defaults to today + 7 days

 */

 function get Calendar Events(params) {

 var daysAhead = params.days_ahead || 7;

 var now = new Date();

 var end = new Date();

 end.set Date(end.get Date() + days Ahead);

 var cal = Calendar App.get Default Calendar();

 var events = cal.getEvents(now, end);

 var results = [];

 for (var i = 0; i < events.length; i++) {

 var ev = events[i];

 results.push({

 title: ev.getTitle(),

 start: ev.get Start Time().toISOString(),

 end: ev.get End Time().toISOString(),

 location: ev.getLocation(),

 description: ev.getDescription().substring(0, 500),

 guests: ev.get Guest List().map(function(g) { return g.get Email(); })

 });

 }

 return { success: true, events: results, count: results.length };

 }

 /**

 * Creates a calendar event.

 * params: { title, start_iso, end_iso, description, guests (array), location }

 */

 function create Calendar Event(params) {

 var cal = Calendar App.get Default Calendar();

 var start = new Date(params.start_iso);

 var end = new Date(params.end_iso);

 var event = cal.createEvent(

 params.title || "Meeting",

 start,

 end,

 {

 description: params.description || "",

 location: params.location || "",

 guests: (params.guests || []).join(","),

 sendInvites: false // Never auto-send invites

 }

 );

 return {

 success: true,

 event_id: event.getId(),

 title: event.getTitle(),

 start: event.get Start Time().to String(),

 link: "https://calendar.google.com/calendar/r"

 };

 }

 /**

 * Logs a sales call as a calendar event with structured notes.

 * params: { contact_name, title, district, date_iso, duration_minutes, notes, outcome, next_steps }

 */

 function logCall(params) {

 var cal = Calendar App.get Default Calendar();

 var start = new Date(params.date_iso || new Date());

 var durationMs = (params.duration_minutes || 30) * 60 * 1000;

 var end = new Date(start.get Time() + duration Ms);

 var eventTitle = "📞 Call — " + (params.contact_name || "Contact") + " @ " + (params.district || "");

 var description = [

 "Contact: " + (params.contact_name || ""),

 "Title: " + (params.title || ""),

 "District: " + (params.district || ""),

 "",

 "Notes:",

 params.notes || "",

 "",

 "Outcome: " + (params.outcome || ""),

 "Next Steps: " + (params.next_steps || ""),

 "",

 "[Logged by Scout]"

 ].join("\n");

 var event = cal.create Event(event Title, start, end, { description: description });

 return {

 success: true,

 event_id: event.getId(),

 title: eventTitle,

 link: "https://calendar.google.com/calendar/r"

 };

 }

 // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

 // SLIDES FUNCTIONS

 // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

 /**

 * Creates a blank presentation.

 * params: { title }

 */

 function createPresentation(params) {

 var pres = SlidesApp.create(params.title || "New Presentation");

 var url = pres.getUrl();

 var id = pres.getId();

 return { success: true, presentation_id: id, url: url, title: pres.getName() };

 }

 /**

 * Creates a district discovery / pitch deck.

 * params: {

 * district_name, state, contact_name, contact_title,

 * student_count, key_pain_points (array), products_to_highlight (array),

 * case_study (str)

 * }

 */

 function create District Deck(params) {

 var district = params.district_name || "School District";

 var state = params.state || "";

 var contact = params.contact_name || "";

 var title = params.contact_title || "";

 var products = params.products_to_highlight || ["Code Combat Classroom", "AI Hack Stack"];

 var painPoints = params.key_pain_points || [];

 var caseStudy = params.case_study || "";

 var deck Title = "Code Combat — " + district;

 var pres = Slides App.create(deck Title);

 var slides = pres.getSlides();

 // ── Slide 1: Title ────────────────────────────────────────

 var slide1 = slides[0];

 slide1.get Shapes()[0].get Text().set Text("Code Combat K-12 CS + AI Suite");

 if (slide1.getShapes().length > 1) {

 slide1.get Shapes()[1].get Text().set Text(district + (state ? ", " + state : "") + "\n" + (contact ? "For: " + contact + (title ? " — " + title : "") : ""));

 }

 // ── Slide 2: The Challenge ────────────────────────────────

 var slide2 = pres.append Slide(Slides App.Predefined Layout.TITLE_AND_BODY);

 slide2.get Shapes()[0].get Text().set Text("The Challenge");

 var challenge Body = pain Points.length > 0

 ? painPoints.map(function(p) { return "• " + p; }).join("\n")

 : "• Engaging students in CS beyond worksheets\n• Teachers without deep CS backgrounds\n• Standards alignment and reporting\n• Limited budget, high expectations";

 slide2.get Shapes()[1].get Text().set Text(challenge Body);

 // ── Slide 3: Our Solution ─────────────────────────────────

 var slide3 = pres.append Slide(Slides App.Predefined Layout.TITLE_AND_BODY);

 slide3.get Shapes()[0].get Text().set Text("The Code Combat Solution");

 slide3.get Shapes()[1].get Text().set Text(

 "Game-based, narrative-driven CS learning that students actually want to do.\n\n" +

 "• Standards-aligned (CSTA, ISTE, CA CS Standards, NGSS)\n" +

 "• Turn-key teacher resources — no CS background needed\n" +

 "• Real code: Python, JavaScript, Lua\n" +

 "• AI literacy built in"

 );

 // ── Slide 4: Products ─────────────────────────────────────

 var slide4 = pres.append Slide(Slides App.Predefined Layout.TITLE_AND_BODY);

 slide4.get Shapes()[0].get Text().set Text("Recommended for " + district);

 var productDescriptions = {

 "CodeCombat Classroom": "Game-based CS in Python/JS/Lua — Grades 6–12",

 "Ozaria": "Narrative RPG CS for middle school",

 "CodeCombat Junior": "Block-based coding — Grades K–5",

 "AI HackStack": "Hands-on AI literacy curriculum",

 "AI Junior": "AI curriculum for K–8",

 "AI League": "Esports coding tournaments",

 "AP CSP Course": "Full AP Computer Science Principles course"

 };

 var productBody = products.map(function(p) {

 return "• " + p + (product Descriptions[p] ? ": " + product Descriptions[p] : "");

 }).join("\n");

 slide4.get Shapes()[1].get Text().set Text(product Body);

 // ── Slide 5: Case Study (if provided) ────────────────────

 if (caseStudy) {

 var slide5 = pres.append Slide(Slides App.Predefined Layout.TITLE_AND_BODY);

 slide5.get Shapes()[0].get Text().set Text("Success Story");

 slide5.get Shapes()[1].get Text().set Text(case Study);

 }

 // ── Slide 6: Next Steps ───────────────────────────────────

 var slide Next = pres.append Slide(Slides App.Predefined Layout.TITLE_AND_BODY);

 slide Next.get Shapes()[0].get Text().set Text("Next Steps");

 slide Next.get Shapes()[1].get Text().set Text(

 "• 30-minute demo — see the platform live\n" +

 "• Free pilot for your teachers\n" +

 "• Custom quote for " + district + "\n\n" +

 "Steven Adkin\nsteven@codecombat.com\ncodecombat.com"

 );

 pres.save And Close();

 return {

 success: true,

 presentation_id: pres.getId(),

 url: "https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/" + pres.getId() + "/edit",

 title: deckTitle,

 slide_count: 5 + (caseStudy ? 1 : 0)

 };

 }

 // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

 // HELPER

 // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

 function json Response(data, status Code) {

 return ContentService

 .create Text Output(JSON.stringify(data))

 .set Mime Type(Content Service.Mime Type.JSON);

 }

 // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

 // GOOGLE DOCS (Phase 5)

 // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

 /**

 * Creates a new Google Doc, optionally moved to a Drive folder.

 * Phase 5+: Used for pre-call briefs and sequence docs.

 * params: { title (str), content (str), folder_id (str, optional) }

 * Returns: { success, doc_id, url, title }

 */

 function create Google Doc(params) {

 var title = params.title || "Scout Doc";

 var content = params.content || "";

 var folderId = params.folder_id || "";

 var doc = DocumentApp.create(title);

 var doc Id = doc.get Id();

 var body = doc.getBody();

 body.setText(content);

 doc.save And Close();

 var url = "https://docs.google.com/document/d/" + docId + "/edit";

 // Move to specified folder — wrapped in try/catch so doc creation never fails

 // even if the folder move hits a permissions issue

 if (folderId) {

 try {

 var file = Drive App.get File ById(doc Id);

 var folder = Drive App.get Folder ById(folder Id);

 folder.addFile(file);

 Drive App.get Root Folder().remove File(file);

 } catch (moveErr) {

 Logger.log("Folder move failed (doc still created): " + move Err.to String());

 }

 }

 return { success: true, doc_id: docId, url: url, title: title };

 }

 /**

 * One-time authorization helper.

 * Run this ONCE from the script editor to grant Google Drive permission,

 * then you can delete it or leave it — it does nothing harmful.

 */

 function test Drive Access() {

 var folder = Drive App.get Folder ById("1n Zh71XWU_TajUVS22F5y StA3_9YNcy3a");

 Logger.log("Drive access OK — folder name: " + folder.getName());

 }

 179 - Em dashes as clause connectors ("we can help — let me know"). Steven uses them occasionally for brief asides only, never as default punctuation between clauses.

 180 - Semicolons. Steven never uses them.

 181 - "I'd be happy to" / "I'd love to" / "Absolutely!" / "Great question!" / "That's a great point"

 182 - - "Want to hop on a quick call" — overused salesy vendor phrase. Use alternatives : "Let's connect via Zoom", "I can show you on a quick Zoom", "Pick a time here and I'll walk you through it" , etc .

 182 + - "Want to hop on a quick call" — overused salesy vendor phrase. Preferred alternative : " Want to connect via Zoom this week? Here's my calendar: [link]". Other good options: " Let's connect via Zoom", "I can show you on a quick Zoom",

 + " Let me show you on a Zoom", " Pick a time here and I'll walk you through it" . Never use #5 style "Easiest way to . .."

 183 - It's always a Zoom call, not a phone call. Say "Zoom" not "call" when referring to meetings.

 ⏺ For step 2, here's exactly what to add:

 In the switch block near line 55, add this case after search_inbox_full :

 case "delete_draft" :

 return json Response(delete Draft(params));

 Then scroll down to after the createDraft function (after line 167), and add this new function:

 /**

 * Deletes a draft by its ID.

 * params: { draft_id (str) }

 */

 function deleteDraft(params) {

 var draftId = params.draft_id;

 if (!draftId) {

 return { success : false , error : "draft_id is required" };

 }

 var drafts = Gmail App.get Drafts();

 for ( var i = 0 ; i < drafts.length; i++) {

 if (drafts[i].get Id() === draft Id) {

 drafts[i].deleteDraft();

 return { success : true , deleted : draftId };

 }

 }

 return { success : false , error : "Draft not found: " + draftId };

 }

 Save with Cmd+S.

 For step 3:

 1. Click Deploy (blue button, top right)

 2. Click Manage deployments

 3. Click the pencil icon (edit) on the existing deployment

 4. In the Version dropdown, select New version

 5. Click Deploy

 ⏺ OK, the Code.gs change is ready. But per the GAS deployment rules, you need to:

 1. Go to script.google.com → Scout Bridge

 2. Paste the updated Code.gs (or just add the deleteDraft function and the case "delete_draft" line in the switch)

 3. Deploy → Manage deployments → pencil icon → Version: New version → Deploy

 The URL stays the same since you're bumping an existing deployment, so no Railway env var update needed.

 Once you've deployed, let me know and I'll delete all 3 orphaned drafts through the GAS bridge. The draft IDs I need to delete are:

 - Kate: r568356451282476298

 - Anthony: r237526669180935243

 - Krista: r-6942613181814878907

 ❯ show me exactly how to do 2 and 3

 ⏺ For step 2, here's exactly what to add:

 In the switch block near line 55, add this case after search_inbox_full :

 case "delete_draft" :

 return json Response(delete Draft(params));

 Then scroll down to after the createDraft function (after line 167), and add this new function:

 /**

 * Deletes a draft by its ID.

 * params: { draft_id (str) }

 */

 function deleteDraft(params) {

 var draftId = params.draft_id;

 if (!draftId) {

 return { success : false , error : "draft_id is required" };

 }

 var drafts = Gmail App.get Drafts();

 for ( var i = 0 ; i < drafts.length; i++) {

 if (drafts[i].get Id() === draft Id) {

 drafts[i].deleteDraft();

 return { success : true , deleted : draftId };

 }

 }

 return { success : false , error : "Draft not found: " + draftId };

 }

 Save with Cmd+S.

 For step 3:

 1. Click Deploy (blue button, top right)

 2. Click Manage deployments

 3. Click the pencil icon (edit) on the existing deployment

 4. In the Version dropdown, select New version

 5. Click Deploy

 That's it. Once you see the "successfully updated" message, tell me and I'll delete those 3 orphaned drafts.

 1. Go to script.google.com → Scout Bridge

 2. Paste the updated Code.gs (or just add the deleteDraft function and the case "delete_draft" line in the switch)

 3. Deploy → Manage deployments → pencil icon → Version: New version → Deploy

 The URL stays the same since you're bumping an existing deployment, so no Railway env var update needed.

 Once you've deployed, let me know and I'll delete all 3 orphaned drafts through the GAS bridge. The draft IDs I need to delete are:

 - Kate: r568356451282476298

 - Anthony: r237526669180935243

 - Krista: r-6942613181814878907

 ❯ show me exactly how to do 2 and 3

 ⏺ For step 2, here's exactly what to add:

 In the switch block near line 55, add this case after search_inbox_full :

 case "delete_draft" :

 return json Response(delete Draft(params));

 Then scroll down to after the createDraft function (after line 167), and add this new function:

 /**

 * Deletes a draft by its ID.

 * params: { draft_id (str) }

 */

 function deleteDraft(params) {

 var draftId = params.draft_id;

 if (!draftId) {

 return { success : false , error : "draft_id is required" };

 }

 var drafts = Gmail App.get Drafts();

 for ( var i = 0 ; i < drafts.length; i++) {

 if (drafts[i].get Id() === draft Id) {

 drafts[i].deleteDraft();

 return { success : true , deleted : draftId };

 }

 }

 return { success : false , error : "Draft not found: " + draftId };

 }

 Save with Cmd+S.

 For step 3:

 1. Click Deploy (blue button, top right)

 2. Click Manage deployments

 3. Click the pencil icon (edit) on the existing deployment

 4. In the Version dropdown, select New version

 5. Click Deploy

 That's it. Once you see the "successfully updated" message, tell me and I'll delete those 3 orphaned drafts.

 ⏺ OK, the Code.gs change is ready. But per the GAS deployment rules, you need to:

 1. Go to script.google.com → Scout Bridge

 2. Paste the updated Code.gs (or just add the deleteDraft function and the case "delete_draft" line in the switch)

 3. Deploy → Manage deployments → pencil icon → Version: New version → Deploy

 The URL stays the same since you're bumping an existing deployment, so no Railway env var update needed.

 Once you've deployed, let me know and I'll delete all 3 orphaned drafts through the GAS bridge. The draft IDs I need to delete are:

 ❯ show me exactly how to do 2 and 3

 ⏺ For step 2, here's exactly what to add:

 In the switch block near line 55, add this case after search_inbox_full :

 case "delete_draft" :

 return json Response(delete Draft(params));

 Then scroll down to after the createDraft function (after line 167), and add this new function:

 /**

 * Deletes a draft by its ID.

 * params: { draft_id (str) }

 */

 function deleteDraft(params) {

 var draftId = params.draft_id;

 if (!draftId) {

 return { success : false , error : "draft_id is required" };

 }

 var drafts = Gmail App.get Drafts();

 for ( var i = 0 ; i < drafts.length; i++) {

 if (drafts[i].get Id() === draft Id) {

 drafts[i].deleteDraft();

 return { success : true , deleted : draftId };

 }

 }

 return { success : false , error : "Draft not found: " + draftId };

 }

 Save with Cmd+S.

 For step 3:

 1. Click Deploy (blue button, top right)

 2. Click Manage deployments

 3. Click the pencil icon (edit) on the existing deployment

 4. In the Version dropdown, select New version

 5. Click Deploy

 That's it. Once you see the "successfully updated" message, tell me and I'll delete those 3 orphaned drafts.

 Once you've deployed, let me know and I'll delete all 3 orphaned drafts through the GAS bridge. The draft IDs I need to delete are:

 - Kate: r568356451282476298

 - Anthony: r237526669180935243

 - Krista: r-6942613181814878907

 ❯ show me exactly how to do 2 and 3

 ⏺ For step 2, here's exactly what to add:

 In the switch block near line 55, add this case after search_inbox_full :

 case "delete_draft" :

 return json Response(delete Draft(params));

 Then scroll down to after the createDraft function (after line 167), and add this new function:

 /**

 * Deletes a draft by its ID.

 * params: { draft_id (str) }

 */

 function deleteDraft(params) {

 var draftId = params.draft_id;

 if (!draftId) {

 return { success : false , error : "draft_id is required" };

 }

 var drafts = Gmail App.get Drafts();

 for ( var i = 0 ; i < drafts.length; i++) {

 if (drafts[i].get Id() === draft Id) {

 drafts[i].deleteDraft();

 return { success : true , deleted : draftId };

 }

 }

 return { success : false , error : "Draft not found: " + draftId };

 }

 Save with Cmd+S.

 For step 3:

 1. Click Deploy (blue button, top right)

 2. Click Manage deployments

 3. Click the pencil icon (edit) on the existing deployment

 4. In the Version dropdown, select New version

 5. Click Deploy

 That's it. Once you see the "successfully updated" message, tell me and I'll delete those 3 orphaned drafts.

 ⏺ The Outreach browser extension is eating the draft body. It's taking over the compose window and stripping my content. Notice it doesn't happen on Krista's thread — probably because she's not in Outreach's system.

 For contacts where the Outreach extension activates, the draft content will get wiped. Two options:

 email-reply-drafting-assistant n______n
 ❯  ( ✦ ✦ )
 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ( oo )

 For contacts where the Outreach extension activates, the draft content will get wiped. Two options:

 1. Quick fix for this one: Just paste this into the compose window that's already open:

 Hey Anthony,

 Sorry about that! Let me look into this for you.

 [STEVEN: Check if this is a licensing issue (free content cap at level 9) or a bug. If licensing, you can unlock more content or give him 30-day trial licenses. If it's a bug, CC support@codecombat.com or Noah.]

 In the meantime, can you tell me how many students you have? I want to make sure we get this sorted out for you quickly.

 2. Long-term fix: We note this as a known limitation — the Outreach extension interferes with API-created drafts for contacts in Outreach. For those contacts, the draft text will need to be shown here in Claude Code for copy-paste instead.

 Want me to add this as a known issue in the workflow instructions?

 ⎿   Added 5 lines, removed 1 line

 58 gmail_create_draft(to=sender_email, body=draft_html, thread Id=thread_id, cc=original_cc_recipients, content Type="text/html")

 59 ```

 61 - **IMPORTANT:** Always use `contentType="text/html"` so links are clickable. Only create ONE draft per thread — never recreate or you'll leave orphaned drafts that show "Draft" labels even after Steven sends.

 61 + **IMPORTANT:**

 62 + - Always use `contentType="text/html"` so links are clickable.

 63 + - Only create ONE draft per thread — never recreate or you'll leave orphaned drafts that show "Draft" labels even after Steven sends.

 64 + - **Outreach extension conflict:** For contacts in Steven's Outreach system, the Outreach browser extension takes over the compose window and wipes API-created draft body text. When this happens, create a standalone draft (no threadId)

 + with a "COPY THIS" subject line so Steven can copy-paste the body into the thread manually. After sending, delete the orphaned threaded draft via GAS bridge `delete_draft` action.

 65 + - If a draft needs to be deleted, use the GAS bridge: `{action: "delete_draft", params: {draft_id: "<id>"}}` via Python requests (curl doesn't handle GAS redirects).

 67 **CC handling:** Include all original CC recipients from the inbound email, minus any noreply/automated addresses. If the inbound was sent to multiple people including Steven, reply-all.

 ⏺ Here's how I'd use it:

 Right now: Say "draft my emails" and I'll batch all 31 unread. I'll read each thread, classify (DRAFT/FLAG/SKIP), and create Gmail drafts for the ones that need replies. You'll get a summary here of what was drafted and what was skipped. Then you go
 to Gmail and work through them — tweak, send, next.

 ❯ It looks like this is still not fixed. [Image #12]

 ⎿   [Image #12]

 ⏺ The Outreach browser extension is eating the draft body. It's taking over the compose window and stripping my content. Notice it doesn't happen on Krista's thread — probably because she's not in Outreach's system.

 For contacts where the Outreach extension activates, the draft content will get wiped. Two options:

 1. Quick fix for this one: Just paste this into the compose window that's already open:

 Hey Anthony,

 Sorry about that! Let me look into this for you.

 [STEVEN: Check if this is a licensing issue (free content cap at level 9) or a bug. If licensing, you can unlock more content or give him 30-day trial licenses. If it's a bug, CC support@codecombat.com or Noah.]

 In the meantime, can you tell me how many students you have? I want to make sure we get this sorted out for you quickly.

 2. Long-term fix: We note this as a known limitation — the Outreach extension interferes with API-created drafts for contacts in Outreach. For those contacts, the draft text will need to be shown here in Claude Code for copy-paste instead.

 Want me to add this as a known issue in the workflow instructions?

 ❯ why did it work fine for krista?

 ⏺ Good question. Let me check — does Krista's thread also show the Outreach toolbar (Assist | Templates | Snippets | Meetings | Reminders | Sequences) when you open the compose window?

 My theory is the Outreach extension only activates on contacts it recognizes from Outreach's database. Anthony is probably in your Outreach system, Krista probably isn't. When Outreach activates on a thread, it takes over the compose window and wipes

 the draft body.

 Can you confirm — does Krista's compose view have that Outreach toolbar or not?

 ⏺ Update ( prompts/reply_draft.md )

 ⎿   Added 5 lines, removed 1 line

 58 gmail_create_draft(to=sender_email, body=draft_html, thread Id=thread_id, cc=original_cc_recipients, content Type="text/html")

 59 ```

 61 - **IMPORTANT:** Always use `contentType="text/html"` so links are clickable. Only create ONE draft per thread — never recreate or you'll leave orphaned drafts that show "Draft" labels even after Steven sends.

 61 + **IMPORTANT:**

 62 + - Always use `contentType="text/html"` so links are clickable.

 63 + - Only create ONE draft per thread — never recreate or you'll leave orphaned drafts that show "Draft" labels even after Steven sends.

 64 + - **Outreach extension conflict:** For contacts in Steven's Outreach system, the Outreach browser extension takes over the compose window and wipes API-created draft body text. When this happens, create a standalone draft (no threadId)

 + with a "COPY THIS" subject line so Steven can copy-paste the body into the thread manually. After sending, delete the orphaned threaded draft via GAS bridge `delete_draft` action.

 65 + - If a draft needs to be deleted, use the GAS bridge: `{action: "delete_draft", params: {draft_id: "<id>"}}` via Python requests (curl doesn't handle GAS redirects).

 67 **CC handling:** Include all original CC recipients from the inbound email, minus any noreply/automated addresses. If the inbound was sent to multiple people including Steven, reply-all.

 ⏺ The Outreach browser extension is eating the draft body. It's taking over the compose window and stripping my content. Notice it doesn't happen on Krista's thread — probably because she's not in Outreach's system.

 For contacts where the Outreach extension activates, the draft content will get wiped. Two options:

 1. Quick fix for this one: Just paste this into the compose window that's already open:

 Hey Anthony,

 Sorry about that! Let me look into this for you.

 [STEVEN: Check if this is a licensing issue (free content cap at level 9) or a bug. If licensing, you can unlock more content or give him 30-day trial licenses. If it's a bug, CC support@codecombat.com or Noah.]

 In the meantime, can you tell me how many students you have? I want to make sure we get this sorted out for you quickly.

 2. Long-term fix: We note this as a known limitation — the Outreach extension interferes with API-created drafts for contacts in Outreach. For those contacts, the draft text will need to be shown here in Claude Code for copy-paste instead.

 Want me to add this as a known issue in the workflow instructions?

 ⏺ Good question. Let me check — does Krista's thread also show the Outreach toolbar (Assist | Templates | Snippets | Meetings | Reminders | Sequences) when you open the compose window?

 My theory is the Outreach extension only activates on contacts it recognizes from Outreach's database. Anthony is probably in your Outreach system, Krista probably isn't. When Outreach activates on a thread, it takes over the compose window and wipes

 the draft body.

 Can you confirm — does Krista's compose view have that Outreach toolbar or not?

 ⎿   Added 5 lines, removed 1 line

 58 gmail_create_draft(to=sender_email, body=draft_html, thread Id=thread_id, cc=original_cc_recipients, content Type="text/html")

 59 ```

 61 - **IMPORTANT:** Always use `contentType="text/html"` so links are clickable. Only create ONE draft per thread — never recreate or you'll leave orphaned drafts that show "Draft" labels even after Steven sends.

 61 + **IMPORTANT:**

 62 + - Always use `contentType="text/html"` so links are clickable.

 63 + - Only create ONE draft per thread — never recreate or you'll leave orphaned drafts that show "Draft" labels even after Steven sends.

 64 + - **Outreach extension conflict:** For contacts in Steven's Outreach system, the Outreach browser extension takes over the compose window and wipes API-created draft body text. When this happens, create a standalone draft (no threadId)

 + with a "COPY THIS" subject line so Steven can copy-paste the body into the thread manually. After sending, delete the orphaned threaded draft via GAS bridge `delete_draft` action.

 65 + - If a draft needs to be deleted, use the GAS bridge: `{action: "delete_draft", params: {draft_id: "<id>"}}` via Python requests (curl doesn't handle GAS redirects).

 67 **CC handling:** Include all original CC recipients from the inbound email, minus any noreply/automated addresses. If the inbound was sent to multiple people including Steven, reply-all.

 58 gmail_create_draft(to=sender_email, body=draft_html, thread Id=thread_id, cc=original_cc_recipients, content Type="text/html")

 61 - **IMPORTANT:** Always use `contentType="text/html"` so links are clickable. Only create ONE draft per thread — never recreate or you'll leave orphaned drafts that show "Draft" labels even after Steven sends.

 61 + **IMPORTANT:**

 62 + - Always use `contentType="text/html"` so links are clickable.

 63 + - Only create ONE draft per thread — never recreate or you'll leave orphaned drafts that show "Draft" labels even after Steven sends.

 64 + - **Outreach extension conflict:** For contacts in Steven's Outreach system, the Outreach browser extension takes over the compose window and wipes API-created draft body text. When this happens, create a standalone draft (no threadId)

 + with a "COPY THIS" subject line so Steven can copy-paste the body into the thread manually. After sending, delete the orphaned threaded draft via GAS bridge `delete_draft` action.

 65 + - If a draft needs to be deleted, use the GAS bridge: `{action: "delete_draft", params: {draft_id: "<id>"}}` via Python requests (curl doesn't handle GAS redirects).

 67 **CC handling:** Include all original CC recipients from the inbound email, minus any noreply/automated addresses. If the inbound was sent to multiple people including Steven, reply-all.

 377 + ## COMPLETED: Email Reply Drafting System (Session 48)

 378 +

 379 + ### What was built

 380 + - **Gmail MCP workflow** — Claude Code reads unread inbox, classifies (DRAFT/FLAG/SKIP), drafts replies in Steven's voice, creates threaded Gmail drafts. Steven opens inbox, tweaks, sends.

 381 + - **Response playbook** (`memory/response_playbook.md`) — 14 categories with real snippets from 150+ sent emails: pricing, budget constraints, scheduling, pilots, support routing, procurement, grants, etc.

 382 + - **Voice profile updated** (`memory/voice_profile.md`) — added Drafting Rules, Anti-AI-Tell Checklist, and 10 Learned Corrections from live testing

 383 + - **Workflow instructions** (`prompts/reply_draft.md`) — full step-by-step for any Claude Code session

 384 + - **GAS bridge `delete_draft`** — added to Code.gs for cleaning up orphaned drafts

 385 + - **Draft log** (`memory/draft_log.md`) — for future "learn from my sends" learning loop

 386 +

 387 + ### Known issues

 388 + - **Outreach browser extension** strips API-created draft body for contacts in Outreach's database. Workaround: create standalone "COPY THIS" draft for copy-paste.

 389 + - Always use `contentType="text/html"` for clickable links

 390 + - Never create duplicate drafts on same thread (causes persistent "Draft" label)

 391 +

 392 + ### Testing results

 393 + - 5 emails drafted during testing, 3 sent successfully. Voice was accurate, content corrections captured as learnings.

 394 + - Banned phrase: "Want to hop on a quick call" — replaced with "Want to connect via Zoom this week?"

 395 + - Pricing rule: push for Zoom first, send pricing after pushback or for international leads

 For contacts where the Outreach extension activates, the draft content will get wiped. Two options:

 1. Quick fix for this one: Just paste this into the compose window that's already open:

 Hey Anthony,

 Sorry about that! Let me look into this for you.

 [STEVEN: Check if this is a licensing issue (free content cap at level 9) or a bug. If licensing, you can unlock more content or give him 30-day trial licenses. If it's a bug, CC support@codecombat.com or Noah.]

 In the meantime, can you tell me how many students you have? I want to make sure we get this sorted out for you quickly.

 2. Long-term fix: We note this as a known limitation — the Outreach extension interferes with API-created drafts for contacts in Outreach. For those contacts, the draft text will need to be shown here in Claude Code for copy-paste instead.

 Want me to add this as a known issue in the workflow instructions?

 the draft body.

 58 gmail_create_draft(to=sender_email, body=draft_html, thread Id=thread_id, cc=original_cc_recipients, content Type="text/html")

 59 ```

 61 - **IMPORTANT:** Always use `contentType="text/html"` so links are clickable. Only create ONE draft per thread — never recreate or you'll leave orphaned drafts that show "Draft" labels even after Steven sends.

 61 + **IMPORTANT:**

 62 + - Always use `contentType="text/html"` so links are clickable.

 63 + - Only create ONE draft per thread — never recreate or you'll leave orphaned drafts that show "Draft" labels even after Steven sends.

 64 + - **Outreach extension conflict:** For contacts in Steven's Outreach system, the Outreach browser extension takes over the compose window and wipes API-created draft body text. When this happens, create a standalone draft (no threadId)

 + with a "COPY THIS" subject line so Steven can copy-paste the body into the thread manually. After sending, delete the orphaned threaded draft via GAS bridge `delete_draft` action.

 65 + - If a draft needs to be deleted, use the GAS bridge: `{action: "delete_draft", params: {draft_id: "<id>"}}` via Python requests (curl doesn't handle GAS redirects).

 67 **CC handling:** Include all original CC recipients from the inbound email, minus any noreply/automated addresses. If the inbound was sent to multiple people including Steven, reply-all.

 Right now: Say "draft my emails" and I'll batch all 31 unread. I'll read each thread, classify (DRAFT/FLAG/SKIP), and create Gmail drafts for the ones that need replies. You'll get a summary here of what was drafted and what was skipped. Then you go

 to Gmail and work through them — tweak, send, next.

 What I can't do yet: Auto-draft as emails arrive in real-time. That would require building a polling system into Scout on Railway, which is possible but a separate project.

 ⎿   Added 49 lines
 730 - `fuzzy_match_name()` uses token subset (2+ token containment = 0.95 score) + Jaccard similarity with 0.7 threshold
 731 - Reengagement overview uses single `get_sequences()` API call (no per-prospect scanning) for fast display
 732 - `_last_reengagement_sequences` global caches overview for Mode 2 index lookup
 733 +
 734 + ---
 735 +
 736 + ## Session 48 (2026-04-07): Email Reply Drafting System

 737 +

 738 + ### What was built

 739 + - **Email Reply Drafting system** — Claude Code workflow using Gmail MCP (`gmail_search_messages`, `gmail_read_thread`, `gmail_create_draft` with `thread Id` + `content Type="text/html"`). Reads unread inbox, classifies emails (DRAFT/FL

 + AG/SKIP), drafts replies in Steven's voice, creates threaded HTML drafts directly in Gmail. Steven's workflow: open inbox → see draft → tweak → send → next.

 740 + - **Response playbook** (`memory/response_playbook.md`) — 14 categories extracted from 150+ real sent emails (100 replies + 50 outbound). Categories: pricing, budget constraints, not ready yet, info gathering, scheduling, free trial/p

 + ilot, no-pressure follow-up, brief acknowledgments, procurement/PO, support routing, unsubscribe, product info, grant funding, referral/handoff. Plus "No Reply Needed" skip list.

 741 + - **Voice profile updated** (`memory/voice_profile.md`) — 3 new sections added: Drafting Rules (pricing call-first preference, no feature fabrication, no combative tone, budget strategy, drive toward calls), Anti-AI-Tell Checklist (13

 + banned patterns), Learned Corrections (10 specific fixes from live testing).

 742 + - **Workflow instructions** (`prompts/reply_draft.md`) — complete step-by-step for any Claude Code session. Includes classification rules, energy matching, CC handling, draft log format, and "learn from my sends" workflow.

 743 + - **GAS bridge `delete_draft`** — new `delete Draft()` function in Code.gs + `case "delete_draft"` route. Uses `Gmail App.get Drafts()` to find by ID, then `.delete Draft()`. Deployed as new version. Called via Python `requests.Session().

 + post()` (curl doesn't handle GAS redirects).

 744 + - **Draft log** (`memory/draft_log.md`) — rolling 14-day log of drafted replies (thread_id, sender, subject, category, full draft text) for future learning loop comparisons.

 745 +

 746 + ### Key decisions

 747 + - **Gmail MCP over GAS bridge for drafting** — Gmail MCP supports `thread Id` for proper threading + `content Type="text/html"` for clickable links. GAS bridge `create_draft` doesn't support threading.

 748 + - **No Python modules needed** — this is a Claude Code workflow, not a Scout/Railway feature. All intelligence lives in memory files (voice profile, playbook) and prompt file (workflow instructions).

 749 + - **Drafts go directly to Gmail** — Steven explicitly requested this. No showing drafts in Claude Code for approval first. Create in Gmail immediately, show brief summary.

 750 + - **Static playbook over dynamic few-shot retrieval** — Steven's inbound categories are predictable (8-10 types cover 90%+ of emails). Static playbook is faster, more reliable, and simpler than searching Gmail for similar past replies

 + at draft time.

 751 +

 752 + ### Testing results (5 emails)

 753 + 1. Kate Grow (AI question) — DRAFT, sent successfully

 754 + 2. Anthony Pollina (locked out of levels) — FLAG, Outreach extension stripped body, used copy-paste workaround

 755 + 3. Krista Farris ("wasn't me") — DRAFT, sent successfully

 756 + 4. Kirk Ellern (budget constraint) — DRAFT, sent successfully

 757 + 5. Maree Pascall (library use case) — DRAFT, created in Gmail

 758 +

 759 + ### Learned corrections (saved to voice profile)

 760 + - Ask specific budget questions, not generic ("under a specific amount?" vs "What does your budget look like?")

 761 + - Add encouragement for budget-constrained prospects ("Don't give up yet!")

 762 + - Mention grant writing specialist when budget is the issue

 763 + - Frame CTAs with incentives ("we'll go over options AND I can set you up with trial licenses")

 764 + - Use "Let's connect via Zoom" not "Want to hop on a quick call" (BANNED — too salesy)

 765 + - Always say "Zoom" not "call" for meetings

 766 + - Keep free license offers flexible ("for a little while" not specific timeframes)

 767 + - Ask scoping questions on separate lines

 768 + - Acknowledge time gaps ("A lot has likely changed since we last connected")

 769 + - Never fabricate product features — use frames if unsure

 770 + - Default to pushing for Zoom before sending pricing (send pricing after pushback or for international)

 771 +

 772 + ### Known issues

 773 + - **Outreach browser extension conflict** — for contacts in Outreach's database, the extension takes over Gmail compose and strips API-created draft body. Workaround: create standalone draft with "COPY THIS" subject for copy-paste. Af

 + ter sending, delete orphaned threaded draft via GAS bridge.

 774 + - **Never create duplicate drafts on same thread** — causes persistent "Draft" label that requires GAS bridge deletion to fix.

 775 + - Gmail MCP has no `delete_draft` tool — must use GAS bridge for cleanup.

 776 +

 777 + ### Implementation notes

 778 + - Two-pass playbook building: Pass 1 surveyed 150 subjects/snippets (lightweight). Pass 2 read 50-70 full threads across key categories.

 779 + - Quality gate: 3+ of 5 test drafts must be usable with minor tweaks before proceeding to learning loop.

 780 + - GAS bridge `delete_draft` iterates all drafts with `Gmail App.get Drafts()` and matches by ID string. Returns `{success, deleted}` or `{success: false, error}`.

 781 + - Python `requests.Session()` handles GAS redirects properly. `urllib` and `curl` do not (302 redirect drops POST body or returns 405).

 58 gmail_create_draft(to=sender_email, body=draft_html, thread Id=thread_id, cc=original_cc_recipients, content Type="text/html")

 59 ```

 61 - **IMPORTANT:** Always use `contentType="text/html"` so links are clickable. Only create ONE draft per thread — never recreate or you'll leave orphaned drafts that show "Draft" labels even after Steven sends.

 61 + **IMPORTANT:**

 62 + - Always use `contentType="text/html"` so links are clickable.

 63 + - Only create ONE draft per thread — never recreate or you'll leave orphaned drafts that show "Draft" labels even after Steven sends.

 64 + - **Outreach extension conflict:** For contacts in Steven's Outreach system, the Outreach browser extension takes over the compose window and wipes API-created draft body text. When this happens, create a standalone draft (no threadId)

 + with a "COPY THIS" subject line so Steven can copy-paste the body into the thread manually. After sending, delete the orphaned threaded draft via GAS bridge `delete_draft` action.

 65 + - If a draft needs to be deleted, use the GAS bridge: `{action: "delete_draft", params: {draft_id: "<id>"}}` via Python requests (curl doesn't handle GAS redirects).

 67 **CC handling:** Include all original CC recipients from the inbound email, minus any noreply/automated addresses. If the inbound was sent to multiple people including Steven, reply-all.

 ⏺ Here's how I'd use it:

 Right now: Say "draft my emails" and I'll batch all 31 unread. I'll read each thread, classify (DRAFT/FLAG/SKIP), and create Gmail drafts for the ones that need replies. You'll get a summary here of what was drafted and what was skipped. Then you go

 to Gmail and work through them — tweak, send, next.

 Daily routine: Do this 2-3 times a day — morning, midday, end of day. Each batch takes me a few minutes to process, then you spend 10-15 minutes in Gmail sending.

 What I can't do yet: Auto-draft as emails arrive in real-time. That would require building a polling system into Scout on Railway, which is possible but a separate project.
